insights.title: OAuth callback processing uses local processed flag to avoid duplicate handling
insights.problem: The component must avoid processing the OAuth callback more than once, but the approach relies on a local React state 'processed' while the effect depends on searchParams. There are subtle risks that the effect can re-run and that the closure captures a stale value of processed.
insights.root_cause: useEffect depends on searchParams and other values but not on 'processed', and the handler uses the closure-captured value of processed. If searchParams changes or the effect re-runs for another reason the initial closure may run again; relying on local state without including it in the deps or using a ref leaves room for double-processing or racey behavior.
insights.solution: Use a ref (useRef) for a mutable 'processed' flag that persists across renders without causing re-renders, or include processed in the effect dependencies and gate execution correctly. Ensure side-effects (router.push and refreshUser) are cancelled/guarded if the component unmounts.
insights.lesson_learned: When preventing duplicate async side-effects in function components, prefer useRef for boolean guards or ensure effect dependency arrays and cleanup logic are correct. Avoid relying on closure-captured state for single-run guarantees.

insights.title: OAuth retry and error handling may swallow errors or retry unexpectedly
insights.problem: The attemptOAuth retries once on error. The retry uses setTimeout to call attemptOAuth(1) but does not guard against component unmount or multiple concurrent invocations. Errors are converted to err.message without checking for structured API error objects.
insights.root_cause: No cancellation token or component-mounted guard; the async retry can run after the component unmounts causing state updates on unmounted components, and error objects may not be plain Error instances.
insights.solution: Use an abort/cancel flag (useRef) and check before performing state updates or navigations. Normalize API errors (e.g., inspect response body) before showing message. Avoid setTimeout without cleanup in effects; store timers and clear them on unmount.
insights.lesson_learned: Always guard async flows and retries with cancellation logic in UI components and normalize errors before using err.message.

insights.title: Logging sensitive values partially but still present in logs
insights.problem: The code logs the OAuth code substring to console: code?.substring(0, 10) + '...'. This reveals part of a sensitive token/code in client logs.
insights.root_cause: Convenience logging was added for debugging without fully redacting tokens.
insights.solution: Avoid logging any part of authorization codes or tokens in production. Use a boolean debug flag and ensure logs are removed or fully redacted before shipping.
insights.lesson_learned: Treat authorization codes and tokens as sensitive; avoid console logging them even partially in client code.

insights.title: maskApiKey can throw when API key length is between 9 and 15 characters
insights.problem: maskApiKey returns key.substring(0, 12) + '•'.repeat(key.length - 16) + key.substring(key.length - 4). If key.length is less than 16 but greater than 8, key.length - 16 is negative which causes String.prototype.repeat to throw a RangeError.
insights.root_cause: Assumption that API keys are at least 16 characters long; the function does not guard against shorter keys which leads to negative repeat count.
insights.solution: Compute mask lengths robustly and guard against negative repeat counts. Example: if key.length <= 12 return a safe masked version (e.g., show first 4 and last 4 with '•' repeated max(0, key.length - 8)), or use Math.max(0, key.length - 8) for repeat count.
insights.lesson_learned: Do not assume external data lengths. When masking secrets, implement robust guards for short values to avoid runtime exceptions.

insights.title: Revoking keys state update is prone to staleness and can leave 'revoking' indicator stuck
insights.problem: The code does: const newRevokingKeys = new Set(revokingKeys); newRevokingKeys.add(keyId); setRevokingKeys(newRevokingKeys); ... finally: const updatedRevokingKeys = new Set(revokingKeys); updatedRevokingKeys.delete(keyId); setRevokingKeys(updatedRevokingKeys); The finally block reads 'revokingKeys' which is stale (state updates are async) so the delete may operate on the wrong set and the UI may remain showing the key as revoking.
insights.root_cause: Using the (stale) revokingKeys state value after scheduling an update; mutating/read-after-write without accounting for React state update async nature.
insights.solution: Use functional state updates that operate on the latest state: setRevokingKeys(prev => { const s = new Set(prev); s.add(keyId); return s; }) and setRevokingKeys(prev => { const s = new Set(prev); s.delete(keyId); return s; }). Alternatively, track the Set instance created earlier and use that instance to remove the id in finally.
insights.lesson_learned: When updating collection-like state (Set/Map/array) across async boundaries, use functional updates or persist the exact instance you intend to mutate to avoid stale reads.

insights.title: Copy to clipboard uses navigator.clipboard without fallback or permission checks
insights.problem: copyToClipboard assumes navigator.clipboard.writeText is available and will succeed. In some browsers or non-secure contexts this may fail causing error to be shown but no fallback is attempted.
insights.root_cause: No feature detection or fallback to older document.execCommand approach.
insights.solution: Feature-detect navigator.clipboard and provide a fallback approach (e.g., temporarily create a textarea, select and execCommand('copy')) or inform users why copy failed and offer manual copy UI.
insights.lesson_learned: Clipboard APIs are not guaranteed across all environments; add graceful fallbacks for better UX.

insights.title: Newly created API key displayed and then dismissed – correct UX but requires caution
insights.problem: The UI shows the newly created key in a panel and instructs the user to copy because the key will not be shown again. If the user dismisses or navigates away, there is no recovery.
insights.root_cause: Backend returns the key only on create and does not allow retrieving the raw key again by design.
insights.solution: Make messaging explicit and consider an optional 'download key' or immediate copy button that is prominent. Consider storing the new key temporarily in a secure clipboard-friendly flow.
insights.lesson_learned: When secrets are shown only once, the UI must make copying/saving extremely clear and fault-tolerant.

insights.title: Polling for balance update after payment uses setInterval without cleanup on unmount
insights.problem: The code sets a setInterval to poll the credits endpoint and a setTimeout to stop polling after 30s. There is no cleanup that clears the timers if the component unmounts earlier, which can lead to timers running after unmount and potential memory leaks or setting state on unmounted components.
insights.root_cause: Timers (setTimeout/setInterval) created inside an effect are not stored in refs and not cleared in a cleanup function returned by useEffect.
insights.solution: Store interval and timeout IDs in refs and clear them in the cleanup function returned from useEffect. Also guard any state updates with a mounted flag or abort mechanism.
insights.lesson_learned: Always clear timers created inside useEffect in the cleanup to prevent updates after unmount and avoid leaks.

insights.title: Payment URL handling replaces history and relies on webhook timing
insights.problem: On payment success the code replaces the URL with window.history.replaceState and waits 1s before starting polling to give webhook time to process. The 1s heuristic may be insufficient or unnecessary depending on backend timing and can lead to missing balance updates or redundant polling.
insights.root_cause: Use of a fixed delay to wait for backend processing instead of an event-driven signal.
insights.solution: Prefer server-side callbacks (webhooks) driving the state change or use robust polling with exponential backoff and bounded retries. If UX requires immediate feedback, show 'processing' state and poll with short intervals and a reasonable timeout while avoiding aggressive frequency.
insights.lesson_learned: Avoid hard-coded timing assumptions for cross-service synchronization; prefer explicit signals or resilient polling strategies.

insights.title: Deposit minimum enforced on client only
insights.problem: handleDeposit enforces a minimum deposit amount (depositAmount < 5) client-side and shows an error toast. If backend does not enforce the same constraint, inconsistent state or unexpected charges may result.
insights.root_cause: Validation implemented only on the frontend; backend contract not visible in this commit.
insights.solution: Ensure backend performs the same validation and returns meaningful errors. Frontend should handle and display backend validation errors gracefully.
insights.lesson_learned: Do not rely solely on client-side validation for business rules; mirror validations server-side and handle server errors.

app/landing-layout.tsx.short_description: A small Next.js/React layout component that wraps landing pages with a header and global styles
app/landing-layout.tsx.description:
LandingLayout is a thin React layout component intended to provide consistent visual scaffolding for landing pages: it sets global layout CSS (full-height flex column), background and text colors, a monospace font, and renders a Header component followed by children inside a main.flex-grow area. The file exists to centralize page chrome for a set of pages (likely landing pages) so those pages don't need to repeat the same wrapper markup and Tailwind utility classes.

Design decisions are intentionally minimal: it relies on Tailwind utility classes for layout/styling, imports a Header component from a shared components directory, and uses React.ReactNode typing for children to keep the component flexible. Because the file name is landing-layout.tsx rather than Next.js' reserved layout.tsx, it appears intended either to be imported directly by landing pages or to be renamed/used by a route-specific layout mechanism — so consumers control how and where this layout is applied. The component is small and focused on UI composition rather than business logic, making it simple to reuse or extend (e.g., adding props for theme or metadata later).

key_constructs.reasoning: This single export encapsulates the landing page chrome: it ensures every consumer gets consistent background, typography, full-height flex layout, and the header. When modifying landing page scaffolding (styling, additional wrappers, accessibility landmarks), change this component to propagate changes across all pages that use it.

app/page.tsx.description:
This file implements the top-level homepage for a Next.js app (app directory). It is a client component ("use client") that shows a marketing/hero section and a right-hand code sample demonstrating the product SDK. The page is responsible for rendering a Header, a two-column layout (text and code sample), conditional action buttons depending on the authentication state, and a stylized code block using react-syntax-highlighter.

It exists to present the public-facing landing experience and to surface the immediate calls-to-action: go to dashboard for authenticated users, or trigger login for unauthenticated visitors. The component reads auth state from an AuthContext (useAuth) and uses that to decide which CTA to render, while guarding UI with a loading flag. The embedded sdkCode constant provides a concrete quickstart example for SDK usage, which is rendered with SyntaxHighlighter and a dark theme. The file is intentionally client-side because of interactive behavior (login button) and usage of browser-only components (react-syntax-highlighter styling and possibly window-dependent auth flows).

app/layout.tsx.short_description: Root Next.js app layout: global metadata, font, auth provider, analytics and UI plumbing.
app/layout.tsx.description:
This file defines the root layout for a Next.js app (app directory). It centralizes global concerns that must wrap every page: page metadata (title/description/favicon), a global Google font (IBM Plex Mono) applied via a CSS variable on the html element, analytics bootstrapping (Google Analytics scripts and a React GoogleAnalytics component), app-level providers (AuthProvider), and a global UI toaster for notifications. It exists to provide a single, consistent composition point for cross-cutting concerns so individual pages/components do not need to re-declare them.

Design choices: metadata is exported as a Next.js Metadata object so Next can use it for server-rendered head content and SEO. The font is loaded via next/font/google and assigned to an HTML CSS variable class on the html element for easy consumption in CSS. Analytics is bootstrapped in two ways: an inline Script element to load gtag.js with the GA_TRACKING_ID, plus a Script block to initialize gtag config and a React component (GoogleAnalytics) rendered inside Suspense. AuthProvider is used to wrap children so authentication state (session, user) is available app-wide. Toaster (from sonner) is rendered once at the root to display notifications anywhere in the app.

Operational notes and interactions: This file is a server component by default (app/layout.tsx in Next.js). It imports client components (AuthProvider, Toaster, GoogleAnalytics) and uses next/script to inject analytics. Those imports must themselves be implemented as client components ('use client') where necessary; otherwise Next will throw client/server boundary errors. GA_TRACKING_ID is expected to be defined where imported from (components/GoogleAnalytics) and will be embedded into the client script — ensure it is not undefined in production. The metadata title/description and favicon are the canonical defaults for the whole app and will be used unless pages override them.

key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizing metadata here ensures consistent SEO and favicon across the app and leverages Next's app router metadata API.

key_constructs.reasoning: Using next/font provides automatic font optimization and a predictable CSS hook (variable/class) to apply the font across the app.

key_constructs.reasoning: RootLayout is the entry point for all pages under the app/ directory in Next.js; editing it affects the whole application's rendering and bootstrapping logic.

key_constructs.purpose: Imported React component that likely handles analytics pageview/event reporting client-side.
key_constructs.reasoning: This component complements the raw gtag script by managing analytics events in React; it's included inside Suspense to allow lazy-loading and avoid blocking the initial render.

key_constructs.reasoning: This value must be present and valid for GA to function. It's injected into next/script so ensure it's available and safe to expose.

pitfalls.consequence: Converts the file to a client component, preventing Next from performing server-side optimizations and possibly breaking server-rendered metadata or performance characteristics.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep layout as server component; instead mark nested components (AuthProvider, Toaster, GoogleAnalytics) as client components using 'use client' at their own file tops.

pitfalls.consequence: Next will error due to client/server boundary violations or hooks used on the server.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure each client component (AuthProvider, Toaster, GoogleAnalytics) begins with the 'use client' directive and only those components use client-side hooks.

pitfalls.consequence: Analytics won't initialize correctly or may inject 'undefined' into the script causing runtime errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Validate GA_TRACKING_ID at build time; provide a safe fallback or guard the Script injection when ID is missing (e.g., only include analytics in production when ID exists).

pitfalls.consequence: Duplicate pageview events and inflated analytics counts.
pitfalls.prevention: Centralize GA initialization here and ensure other components only call gtag event/metrics functions rather than re-injecting the base script.

components/BlogContent.tsx.short_description: React component that renders HTML blog content and replaces <pre><code> blocks with a SyntaxHighlighter UI.
components/BlogContent.tsx.description:
BlogContent is a client-side React component intended to take pre-generated HTML (usually from markdown -> HTML conversion) and render it inside the app while converting code blocks (
...
) into a styled SyntaxHighlighter block. It exists to improve the appearance and UX of code in posts by using react-syntax-highlighter (atomOneDark style) and a custom wrapper UI with 'traffic light' window controls. The component uses a helper (processContent) to split the incoming HTML string into segments that are either code blocks or regular HTML. Code blocks are HTML-decoded (basic entities), trimmed and then rendered inside a SyntaxHighlighter component with consistent styling; non-code parts are rendered using dangerouslySetInnerHTML. The file intentionally marks itself as a client component ("use client") — this is necessary because react-syntax-highlighter depends on browser-only features (and to avoid SSR mismatches when using certain DOM APIs/styles). The implementation favors simplicity (regex splitting) over a full HTML parse, which is pragmatic for many markdown->HTML use cases but has known fragility against arbitrary HTML inputs. Design decisions: the author chose regex-based splitting for simplicity and performance over a heavier DOM parse or HTML parser dependency. Code language detection is not implemented (language hardcoded to 'python'), and entity decoding covers only a small set of common entities. Styling is done by combining Tailwind-style classes and inline customStyle passed to SyntaxHighlighter. The component's surface is a single prop: content (string), which keeps the component focused and easy to reuse. Practical fit: This component sits at the rendering boundary for blog/article content, typically used by a post page component that converts markdown to HTML and passes it down. Because it handles HTML directly and uses dangerouslySetInnerHTML, it should be used only with trusted/clean HTML (e.g., sanitized server-side or produced by the app's markdown pipeline).

key_constructs.purpose: Local helper that splits the HTML string into code and non-code fragments, decodes basic HTML entities in code blocks, and returns an array of React nodes mixing SyntaxHighlighter-wrapped code blocks and HTML segments rendered with dangerouslySetInnerHTML.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function encapsulates the transformation logic. If making changes to parsing, language detection, or entity decoding, this is the function to modify. It's also the hotspot for potential bugs related to HTML parsing and XSS.

replaces 
...
blocks with a SyntaxHighlighter instance
decodes a small set of HTML entities inside code blocks
wraps non-code HTML fragments using dangerouslySetInnerHTML

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on regex-splitting to parse arbitrary HTML
pitfalls.consequence: Malformed or unusual HTML (nested pre/code, attributes with > or < inside, or unexpected whitespace) can break detection and lead to incorrect rendering or lost content.
pitfalls.prevention: Use a proper HTML parser (DOMParser in browser or parse5 server-side) or ensure the markdown-to-HTML pipeline always produces predictable code block markup.

pitfalls.mistake: Passing unsanitized HTML to the content prop
pitfalls.consequence: Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities because the component uses dangerouslySetInnerHTML for non-code fragments.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure HTML is sanitized before being passed in (server-side sanitization or a trusted markdown renderer). Treat BlogContent as only for trusted content.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming inline style objects can include '!important' declarations
pitfalls.consequence: CodeTagProps style entries like 'background: "transparent !important"' are invalid for React style objects and will be ignored or cause unexpected behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Avoid '!important' in inline style objects; instead, rely on CSS classes, higher-specificity selectors, or styled components to override styles.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming all code blocks are python
pitfalls.consequence: Incorrect syntax highlighting for other languages leading to poor developer UX.
pitfalls.prevention: Extract language from the code block's class attribute (e.g., class="language-js") or enhance the markdown pipeline to inject language info into the HTML.

JSX return in BlogContent: wrapper classNames and where processed nodes are injected
skip_unless_needed: Tailwind class names for styling if only changing parsing logic
minor style details inside customStyle passed to SyntaxHighlighter

components/Footer.tsx.short_description: A small React/Next.js footer component rendering site links and the current year.
components/Footer.tsx.description:
This file defines a presentational Footer React component used to render the bottom site chrome. It exists to centralize the site's footer UI (copyright text and navigational links) so pages/layouts can include it consistently. The component uses Tailwind CSS utility classes for styling and Next.js' Link component for internal navigation.

Design decisions: it's a purely presentational functional component (no props), which keeps it simple and easy to include in a global layout (e.g., app/layout.tsx or pages/_app.tsx). It computes the copyright year dynamically with new Date().getFullYear(), and contains a couple of commented-out links suggesting iterative product/marketing changes (pricing, book a demo). The component uses a responsive flex layout to adapt between column (mobile) and row (desktop) arrangements.

How it fits: this is a small building-block UI component intended to be imported by the global layout or individual pages. It's not stateful and does not depend on external data, making it straightforward to test or to replace. The use of Next/Link indicates the app uses Next.js routing; Tailwind classes indicate a Tailwind-based design system.

key_constructs.reasoning: When working on layouts or pages, this is the single export to include for consistent footer UI. It's a pure functional component with no props so changes here affect every layout that imports it — be mindful when modifying styles, classNames, or the link set.

displays the current year dynamically
provides navigational links (Home, Team, Contact)

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming new Date().getFullYear() always shows the correct current year in all Next.js rendering modes
pitfalls.consequence: If the page is statically generated at build time (SSG) the year will be frozen to the build year until a rebuild; client-side hydration will match server HTML but incremental static regeneration or long-lived builds can show stale years.
pitfalls.prevention: If you need a guaranteed up-to-the-second year, compute it client-side (useEffect to set state) or use server-side rendering on each request. For most apps the build-time year is acceptable.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying Link usage without checking Next.js version
pitfalls.consequence: In older Next versions, Link expects an <a> child; passing className directly on Link (as here) may not work in those versions leading to broken styling or inaccessible links.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure the project's Next.js version supports className on Link (Next 12+ supports, but check conventions). If targeting older conventions, wrap content with <a> and put className on the anchor.

pitfalls.mistake: Uncommenting commented-out external links and not adding target/rel attributes
pitfalls.consequence: External links opened without rel='noopener noreferrer' or target attributes can be a security/UX issue (opening in same tab unexpectedly).
pitfalls.prevention: When reintroducing external links, add proper attributes and consider whether they belong in the footer.

components/ConveyorBeltAnimation.tsx.short_description: React component that renders an animated conveyor-belt UI of issue items processed by an AI agent, with performance and responsive optimizations.
components/ConveyorBeltAnimation.tsx.description:
ConveyorBeltAnimation is a client-side React component (Next.js "use client") that visualizes GitHub-like issue items traveling across a conveyor belt toward an AI "processor". It exists to provide a visually engaging UI/marketing widget showing items spawning, moving across a belt, getting flagged as processed when they reach a processor location, glowing, and then being removed. The component uses framer-motion for fluid animations, lucide-react icons for repository icons, and Tailwind CSS for styling.

The file is designed with responsiveness (isMobile breakpoints) and runtime performance in mind. It limits spawn rates and maximum items on mobile, disables non-critical animations on small screens, and uses a custom animation update loop via framer-motion's useAnimationFrame to do batch position updates, frame skipping, and batched removals (itemsToRemoveRef) rather than many state updates per frame. The component also memoizes static arrays and animation config objects (repositories, issueTitles, beltAnimation, rollerAnimation, processorAnimation, etc.) to avoid unnecessary re-renders and allocations.

Rendering is split into background grid, belt sections (animated sliding background), rollers, the AI processor bubble with glow, and the moving issue boxes. Individual issue items are stored in state as objects with id, repository, title, processed flag, and position. They are spawned on an interval, move according to a speed factor derived from deltaTime and container width, and are marked processed when within pixel-distance and threshold relative to the processor. Processed items are queued for removal after a timeout and animated with different motion states. The component trades precision for CPU usage by skipping frames, coalescing updates, and dropping off-screen items early in rendering.

This file fits into the larger system as a purely presentational/interactive UI component meant to be embedded in a page (e.g., marketing/home page). It does not own persisted data nor exposes an API surface beyond its default exported React component. Integration points to check when modifying it are its container (parent) sizing, tailwind theme/utility classes, and packages framer-motion and lucide-react. There are a few non-obvious behaviors and subtle bugs to be aware of (see pitfalls).

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming the setInterval created inside the setTimeout is cleaned up by returning a function from the setTimeout callback.
pitfalls.consequence: The interval continues running after the component unmounts or effect re-runs, leaking timers and spawning items indefinitely (memory and CPU leak).
pitfalls.prevention: Store the interval id in a ref or outer variable and clearInterval in the useEffect cleanup. Don't rely on returning a cleanup from inside the setTimeout callback.

pitfalls.mistake: Using Date.now() as the sole source of id without additional uniqueness safeguards.
pitfalls.consequence: Rapid item creation could produce non-unique ids (if multiple items created within the same millisecond), which breaks React keying and AnimatePresence/layoutId uniqueness assumptions.
pitfalls.prevention: Use a monotonic counter (ref increment), UUID, or combine Date.now with a per-tick counter to guarantee uniqueness.

pitfalls.mistake: Relying solely on offsetWidth from the containerRef updated only on window resize.
pitfalls.consequence: If the parent layout changes size without a window resize (e.g., CSS transitions, dynamic content, or different layout contexts), containerWidth may be stale, causing incorrect movement/processing detection.
pitfalls.prevention: Consider using ResizeObserver to detect element size changes or recalculate on mount and when related props change.

pitfalls.mistake: Extensive use of layout animations (layoutId/layout) while keeping many elements in state.
pitfalls.consequence: Potential jank on lower-end devices due to heavy layout calculations and reflows from framer-motion layout transitions.
pitfalls.prevention: Limit layout animations to fewer elements or disable layout transitions on mobile; rely on transform-based animations (translate/opacity) where possible.

useAnimationFrame callback : frame loop, position update, processed detection, and batched removals
render return JSX : how belts, rollers, processor, and DataItem render logic and animations are composed
getRepoIcon/getRepoColor : mapping of repo path to visuals

components/GoogleAnalytics.tsx.short_description: Client-side Next.js component and helpers for sending Google Analytics (gtag) pageviews and events.
components/GoogleAnalytics.tsx.description:
This file provides a small client-side utility for integrating Google Analytics (GA4 via gtag) into a Next.js app. It defines the tracking id constant, two helper functions (pageview and event) that wrap window.gtag calls, and a React client component (GoogleAnalytics) that uses Next.js navigation hooks to fire a pageview whenever the pathname or search params change. The intent is to centralize GA interaction so other parts of the app can import pageview/event helpers and include the component in a layout so automatic pageview tracking happens on route changes.

Design-wise the module is intentionally minimal: it guards calls to window.gtag (making the wrappers no-ops when the gtag script hasn't been loaded) and relies on next/navigation hooks (usePathname, useSearchParams) inside a "use client" component to detect navigation changes in the Next.js App Router. The component returns null because its only job is side-effects (sending pageview events). This separation (helpers + lightweight component) makes it easy to reuse the event/pageview functions in other places (e.g., on-click analytics) without mounting the component multiple times.

It fits into the larger app by being mounted at a high level (commonly in app/layout.tsx or a root-level component) and by assuming a GA gtag script is added somewhere else (the file does not add the <script> tag that loads gtag). Consumers should ensure the gtag script is loaded and that GA_TRACKING_ID matches the one used when loading the script. The file also contains a subtle bug/UX tradeoff in how it concatenates pathname and query string (see pitfalls) which can produce malformed page_path values unless corrected.

key_constructs.name: GA_TRACKING_ID
key_constructs.purpose: Holds the Google Analytics measurement ID used when sending config and event calls (e.g., 'G-XXXXXXX').
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the ID so the same value is used by pageview, event helpers, and potentially other modules. If the ID changes, update this single constant or replace it with an env-driven value.

key_constructs.name: pageview
key_constructs.purpose: Sends a 'config' gtag call to record a page view for the provided url.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates the gtag 'config' call and guards against calling window.gtag if it is not defined. Use this from route-change handlers or manual navigation tracking.

key_constructs.name: event
key_constructs.purpose: Sends a generic gtag 'event' with action, category, label, and value properties.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a simple structured wrapper for sending analytics events throughout the app. The argument is typed as any here; callers should pass a consistent shape.

key_constructs.name: GoogleAnalytics
key_constructs.purpose: A React client component that uses Next.js navigation hooks to fire pageview(url) when pathname or searchParams change.
key_constructs.reasoning: Acts as a single mounted side-effect component to automatically emit pageviews on client-side navigation in Next's App Router. Returning null signals it's purely for side effects.

key_behaviors: sends a pageview event on client route changes
returns early when gtag is not available to avoid runtime errors
exposes an event() helper for sending structured event data
is a no-UI side-effect component (returns null)

pitfalls.mistake: Concatenating pathname and searchParams directly (pathname + searchParams.toString())
pitfalls.consequence: Produces URLs that lack the '?' between pathname and query string (e.g., '/pageparam=value' instead of '/page?param=value'), causing inaccurate page_path values sent to GA.
pitfalls.prevention: Use: const qs = searchParams.toString(); const url = pathname + (qs ? `?${qs}` : '');

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming window.gtag exists because the script is included elsewhere
pitfalls.consequence: pageview and event silently no-op if the gtag script isn't loaded, so analytics data will be missing without any visible error.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure the gtag script tag (with the same GA_TRACKING_ID) is loaded in the app's root (e.g., app/layout.tsx) before this component mounts; consider adding a debug log or optional callback to surface missing gtag in dev builds.

pitfalls.mistake: Importing/using this component in a server-rendered context or forgetting 'use client'
pitfalls.consequence: Accessing next/navigation hooks or window in SSR will fail; the file already declares 'use client' which is required.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep 'use client' directive and only mount this component inside client-rendered areas (root layout is fine in App Router).

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on the loose any typing of event()
pitfalls.consequence: Inconsistent event payloads across the app leading to analytics events difficult to query or unreliable data.
pitfalls.prevention: Introduce a typed event interface and validate or standardize fields before sending events.

event: wrapper around window.gtag('event', ...)

components/theme-provider.tsx.short_description: A thin client-side wrapper that exposes next-themes' ThemeProvider for the app.
components/theme-provider.tsx.description:
This file defines and exports a small React component named ThemeProvider that simply forwards its props to next-themes' ThemeProvider (imported as NextThemesProvider). The `use client` directive at the top makes this explicit that the component must run on the client, which is required because next-themes interacts with browser APIs (localStorage/window) to persist theme preferences. The module exists to centralize the theme-provider boundary in one place so the app's root layout (or similar) can import a consistent provider and so future cross-cutting behavior (custom defaults, logging, theme mapping, or additional props) can be added without changing many call sites.

Because it only delegates to NextThemesProvider, the file is intentionally minimal. It reuses the ThemeProviderProps type from next-themes, which keeps the surface area identical to the upstream provider and makes it straightforward to pass props such as defaultTheme, storageKey, attribute, or enableSystem. The component is typically used at the top-level of a React tree (for example app/layout.tsx or pages/_app.tsx) so that all client components can read and toggle theme state via next-themes hooks or context consumers. The wrapper also enforces the client-side execution requirement and serves as the single place to change theme-related defaults for the whole app.

key_constructs.name: ThemeProvider
key_constructs.purpose: Client-side wrapper component that forwards props to next-themes' ThemeProvider.
key_constructs.reasoning: Keeping a tiny centralized wrapper makes it easy to control theme behavior application-wide (for defaults, telemetry, or extra props) and ensures the `use client` directive is present. An agent editing theme behavior should update this component so changes propagate everywhere that uses the provider.

key_behaviors: provides theme context to descendant components
forwards next-themes props such as defaultTheme and enableSystem
ensures provider runs on the client

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the 'use client' directive or converting this to a server component
pitfalls.consequence: next-themes will attempt to use browser APIs on the server, causing runtime errors or mismatched rendering.
pitfalls.prevention: Always keep 'use client' at the top and ensure this component is only imported into client trees or top-level layouts that support client components.

pitfalls.mistake: Wrapping the app with multiple ThemeProvider instances
pitfalls.consequence: Context collision or unexpected theme behavior due to nested providers overriding each other.
pitfalls.prevention: Use this single centralized provider at the application root; avoid adding other next-themes providers elsewhere.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming persisted theme state is available immediately on first render
pitfalls.consequence: Flash of wrong theme (FOUC) may occur because next-themes hydrates from localStorage after first render.
pitfalls.prevention: Consider next-themes' recommended attributes/config (like attribute='class') or server-side strategies to avoid FOUC; review next-themes docs before changing defaults.

import lines: shows dependency on next-themes and the ThemeProviderProps type

components/Header.tsx.short_description: Responsive site header with navigation links and auth-aware sign in / dashboard UI.
components/Header.tsx.description:
This is a client-side Next.js React component that renders the top site header/navigation. It exists to provide a persistent, responsive navigation bar that shows site links, the company logo, and authentication-aware controls (Sign In / Dashboard / Logout). The component uses local state to manage a mobile drop-down menu and consumes an AuthContext (useAuth) to determine whether to show login, logout, or the user's name and dashboard link.

Design-wise it is a small presentational + container component: most logic is UI state (mobile menu open/close) and reacting to auth state (user/loading/login/logout). The component is explicitly a client component ("use client") because it uses hooks (useState, useAuth) and DOM event handlers. It uses Next.js Link and Image components, Tailwind CSS utility classes for styling and responsiveness, and icons from lucide-react. The file contains markup for both the desktop navigation (visible from md and up) and a mobile menu that conditionally renders when a boolean state is true. Links in the mobile menu close the menu on click; auth actions in the mobile menu also close it after calling the login/logout handlers.

pitfalls.consequence: Next.js may block the external image or throw an error if domain not allowed in next.config.js.

pitfalls.mistake: Not handling keyboard accessibility for the mobile menu (no aria, escape key, focus trap).
pitfalls.consequence: Poor accessibility (screen readers and keyboard users can't reliably control the menu).
pitfalls.prevention: Add aria-expanded/aria-controls to the toggle button, provide sr-only text, handle Escape to close, and consider focus trapping when menu is open.

pitfalls.mistake: Calling login/logout but not waiting for or handling errors.
pitfalls.consequence: User could stay with mobile menu open or interface in an inconsistent state if auth call fails.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure login/logout return promises or expose success/failure, and update UI accordingly (e.g., loading indicator, close menu only after success or always close with optimistic UI).

components/SampleDetailsModal.tsx.short_description: React modal component to display a Sample's metadata, test lists, code patches and highlighted diffs.
components/SampleDetailsModal.tsx.description:
This file defines a client-side React component (TypeScript + JSX) that renders a detailed modal for a Sample domain object. It exists to centralize the UX/UI for inspecting a sample: metadata (repo, commit, version, verifier, created time), categorized test results with expandable lists (fail->pass, pass->pass, fail->fail), code patches (diffs) and full code blocks with syntax highlighting, and hint text. The modal uses shared UI primitives (Dialog, DialogContent, DialogHeader, DialogTitle, Badge) and third-party libs for icons (lucide-react) and code highlighting (react-syntax-highlighter).

The design favors a single composable file with small subcomponents to keep rendering logic near the markup: parseDiff for line-level diff parsing, DiffHighlighter to display diffs with color-coded rows and line numbers, CodeHighlighter using react-syntax-highlighter to show formatted code, and TestList to render expandable lists for test groups. State is local (expandedTests) and only controls the expand/collapse of test group panes. The component returns null early if no sample is provided, avoiding rendering the dialog markup prematurely. It receives isOpen and onClose props and wires them to the Dialog component.

Why it exists: to give engineers and users a single place to inspect the full details of a sample and its behavioral and code-level changes in a readable, color-coded UI. It fits into the app's UI layer and relies on a Sample type from lib/api and shared UI primitives, so changes to the sample shape or dialog primitives will require edits here. The component is opinionated about appearance (Tailwind classes) and about how diffs are parsed and presented (simple per-line parse and classing based on leading characters), making it easy to understand but limited to unified-style diff lines beginning with +, -, @@ or plain context.

contexts/AuthContext.tsx.short_description: React client-side authentication context providing user state, login/logout, and refresh logic.
contexts/AuthContext.tsx.description:
This file implements a client-side React context that centralizes authentication state for the application. It exists to provide a single source of truth for the currently authenticated User, a loading indicator while user information is being fetched, and helper actions for login, logout, and refreshing the user. The AuthProvider fetches the current user once on mount and exposes functions that wrap the underlying AuthService (from '@/lib/auth'), including redirecting the browser to GitHub's OAuth URL to initiate login and calling AuthService.logout for sign-out.

The design keeps AuthService concerns (API calls, token storage) separated in '@/lib/auth' and uses React Context to make user and authentication actions available to any component tree under AuthProvider. The component is marked with "use client" so it runs only in the browser — important because it uses window.location and client-only lifecycle hooks. The useAuth hook enforces that consumer components are nested under an AuthProvider by throwing an error if used outside the provider, which helps catch integration mistakes early.

Key behaviors include an initial refresh of the user on mount (with loading state), safe error handling when fetching the user (errors are logged and user is set to null), and simple login/logout flows. Errors from AuthService.getCurrentUser are swallowed after logging which makes UI resilient but may hide transient issues; refreshUser is exposed so consumers can trigger revalidation when needed (e.g., after OAuth callback handling).

key_constructs.reasoning: Simplifies usage in components and provides a developer-friendly error if the provider is missing, preventing silent failures and null checks across the app.

key_constructs.reasoning: Implements a simple redirect-based OAuth initiation; if you need to change auth providers or add client-side UI flows (e.g., popup), update this function.

key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates sign-out side effects; if logout needs more cleanup (e.g., clearing application caches), this is the single place to extend.

hooks/use-mobile.tsx.short_description: A small React hook that reports whether the viewport is considered mobile (<768px).
hooks/use-mobile.tsx.description:
Provides a reusable React hook useIsMobile() that determines whether the current browser viewport should be considered "mobile" based on a single breakpoint (768px). It exists to centralize responsive logic so components can render differently for mobile vs. desktop without duplicating matchMedia logic across the codebase. The hook sets up a MediaQueryList change listener so the boolean updates when the viewport crosses the breakpoint.

Design decisions: the breakpoint is a single constant (MOBILE_BREAKPOINT = 768) making it easy to change project-wide behavior from one place. The hook initializes state as undefined and then updates it within a useEffect that only runs in the browser (so no direct window access on the server during render). It returns a coerced boolean (!!isMobile) so callers always get a boolean value instead of undefined. This design favors a deterministic boolean API for consumers even though the initial render may be false until the effect runs.

How it fits: UI components that need to conditionally render mobile-specific layout (navbars, menus, sidebars, etc.) can call useIsMobile() and adapt rendering. Because it relies on window.matchMedia/window.innerWidth, it is inherently tied to client-side rendering and browser environment behavior.

key_constructs.name: MOBILE_BREAKPOINT
key_constructs.purpose: Defines the width threshold (768px) used to decide mobile vs desktop.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the breakpoint value so all consumers of the hook use the same threshold. Changing it here will update behavior globally for this hook.

key_constructs.name: useIsMobile
key_constructs.purpose: React hook that returns true when the viewport width is less than MOBILE_BREAKPOINT, and updates on viewport changes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates media query lifecycle and state management so components have a simple boolean to branch rendering. It sets up and cleans up a MediaQueryList change listener inside a useEffect and uses window.innerWidth for the initial and subsequent checks.

key_behaviors: determines if viewport width is less than 768px
registers a media query change listener to update state on resize
returns a boolean value to consumer components

pitfalls.mistake: Assume useIsMobile returns the correct value during the first render (server or immediate client render).
pitfalls.consequence: Initial render will receive false (because undefined is coerced to false) which can lead to hydration mismatches in SSR or brief incorrect UI state on initial paint.
pitfalls.prevention: If SSR-consistent rendering is required, explicitly handle undefined state in consumers or change the hook to return `undefined | boolean` (avoid coercing) and let callers render placeholders until the actual value is known.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume addEventListener/removeEventListener exists on MediaQueryList in all target browsers.
pitfalls.consequence: Older browsers (and some environments) use mql.addListener / mql.removeListener; addEventListener may throw or the listener won't be called/removed, causing memory leaks or missed updates.
pitfalls.prevention: Polyfill or conditionally use addEventListener if present, otherwise fall back to addListener/removeListener.

pitfalls.mistake: Use window.* APIs outside of useEffect or without guarding for non-browser environments.
pitfalls.consequence: Server-side rendering will fail if window is accessed during render.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep window access inside useEffect (as done here) or guard with typeof window !== 'undefined' if needed elsewhere.

key_sections: useIsMobile: main hook, returns coerced boolean for UI usage
useEffect block: sets up MediaQueryList, registers onChange, sets initial state and cleans up listener

app/blog/page.tsx.short_description: Next.js app directory server component rendering the blog index (list of posts).
app/blog/page.tsx.description:
This file implements the blog listing page for a Next.js (app router) application. It exports a server component function BlogPage which retrieves all blog posts via getAllPosts() (imported from '@/lib/blog') and renders them inside a shared LandingLayout. The page shows a headline, an optional descriptive intro, a fallback message when there are no posts, and an article list when posts exist. Each article shows a formatted date, title (linked to /blog/[slug]), excerpt, and a "Read more →" link.

The file exists to provide a centralized, server-rendered index of blog posts. By calling getAllPosts() at render time it keeps the page data-driven and suitable for static generation or server rendering depending on the Next.js configuration. The choice to keep this page a plain server component (no "use client") allows server-only operations like filesystem access or synchronous data loading in getAllPosts, and reduces client bundle size. The component focuses on presentation and delegates post retrieval to the lib layer and layout concerns to LandingLayout, keeping responsibilities separated.

Important design points: dates are formatted using toLocaleDateString('en-US') ensuring a consistent human-readable date format; Link components are used for client-side navigation to per-post pages; a clear empty-state is included. The component assumes getAllPosts returns an array of post objects with at least slug, date, title, and excerpt properties. If you change the post shape or want different sorting/filtering, adjust getAllPosts or post mapping logic here. Also check LandingLayout for any shared page frame/styling changes that would affect this page.

app/contact/page.tsx.description:
This file defines the Contact page for a Next.js app (app-directory style) and exists to provide a simple contact surface by embedding a Google Form in an iframe. It composes site chrome (Header and Footer components) around a main content area that uses Tailwind utility classes for layout and styling. The file purposefully delegates form handling to Google Forms rather than building an internal contact submission flow, simplifying backend requirements and offloading form processing/storage to Google.

Design choices: the file is a small functional React component exported as the default component for the route. It keeps responsibilities minimal: layout composition and embedding the remote form. Tailwind classes manage spacing and a mobile-responsive container; however the iframe uses a fixed height (1250px) and default iframe attributes. This file fits into the larger system as a static route under /contact that relies on shared Header/Footer components for consistent site navigation and footer content. There are no tests provided in the repository for this page, so behavioral verification is likely done via end-to-end or manual testing when running the app.

app/dashboard/layout.tsx.short_description: Client-side Next.js layout component that enforces auth and renders the dashboard chrome (header, sidebar, content).
app/dashboard/layout.tsx.description:
This file defines a client-side Next.js layout component used for all routes under /dashboard. It exists to provide the shared dashboard UI shell (top header with user info/docs/logout, left navigation, and the main content region where child pages render) and to centralize authentication gating for the entire dashboard subtree. By placing authentication logic here, every dashboard page inherits the same guard: while auth state is loading a spinner is rendered; if the user is unauthenticated the client router redirects to the public root; if authenticated the layout and children are rendered.

Design choices: The file uses the "use client" directive so it can call React hooks (useEffect) and Next.js client router (useRouter). It consumes a useAuth context (imported from '@/contexts/AuthContext') to get user, loading, and logout — this keeps auth concerns centralized in a context implementation and keeps the layout focused on UX/flow. The layout composes simple presentational pieces (header, nav links, logout button) using Tailwind classes and lucide-react icons. It intentionally returns null when no user is present (after loading ends) to avoid flickering UI before redirect completes.

How it fits: This is the outer wrapper for all dashboard pages; any dashboard-specific pages/components should be children of this layout and therefore automatically receive the consistent layout and auth behavior. It references the AuthContext and uses next/navigation router; changes to authentication, redirect behavior, or nav items will affect the entire dashboard subtree. The file is small and focuses on UX, not business logic; it expects the auth context to provide correct user/loading/logout semantics.

app/dashboard/page.tsx.short_description: Client-side Next.js dashboard page that shows user account, API keys and credit balance.
app/dashboard/page.tsx.description:
This is a client component (note the "use client" directive) that renders the user dashboard UI for logged-in users. It reads the authenticated user from AuthContext (useAuth) and fetches billing/credit information from an ApiService. The component exists to present a compact overview: active API keys count and previews, credits balance with skeleton loading state, account status and member since date, and last-login information. It centralizes several small UI responsibilities (data fetching for credits, formatting dates and currencies, and rendering summary cards) so the rest of the dashboard sub-pages (API keys, credits management) can be navigated to from here.

Design-wise the file keeps data fetching minimal and localized: it only fetches UserCredits, relying on the AuthContext to supply the user and their api_keys. The useEffect hook loads credits when a user becomes available and sets up a visibilitychange listener to refresh credits when the user returns from a potential external payment flow. Presentation is implemented with small helper functions (formatDate, formatCurrency) and straightforward conditional rendering for loading, success and error states. Because it is a client component it directly interacts with the DOM (document.hidden), Intl APIs and runtime state hooks.

This file fits into the larger app as the main dashboard landing page under /dashboard. It depends on AuthContext for user data, ApiService for billing API calls, and routes like /dashboard/api-keys and /dashboard/credits for navigation. It is UI-focused and intentionally lightweight in business logic: it defers authoritative operations (like creating or disabling API keys or performing payments) to other pages or backend services.

app/datasets/page.tsx.short_description: React client page that lists datasets and paginated samples with search and a sample details modal.
app/datasets/page.tsx.description:
This file defines a client-side React page (Next.js 'use client') that lets users browse available datasets, select one, and view a paginated list of samples for the selected dataset. It exists to provide a searchable, paginated dataset explorer UI integrated with the application's ApiService. The component fetches the list of datasets on mount and then fetches samples whenever the selected dataset, page, page size, or debounced search query changes.

The page handles common dataset listing UX: selecting datasets, showing dataset metadata, searching samples with client-side debouncing, paginating results (including page size selection), showing loading states and empty states, and opening a SampleDetailsModal when the user clicks a sample. It uses an external ApiService (imported from '@/lib/api') to retrieve datasets and samples, and uses SampleDetailsModal and LandingLayout components to integrate into the rest of the UI.

Design decisions: - The component keeps local state for selectedDataset, pagination (currentPage, pageSize), search/debouncedSearchQuery, and loading flags. - Search input is debounced with a 500ms timer to reduce API calls. - Pagination UI shows up to 5 page buttons and previous/next controls; page size changes reset to page 1. - Language badges are decorated with Tailwind CSS class strings chosen by getLanguageColor. - Error conditions are surfaced to users via toast.error and logged to console. The file is purely presentational + orchestration and expects API and modal components to carry heavy lifting for data shapes and detailed rendering of a sample.

app/team/page.tsx.short_description: Next.js React component that renders a static 'Meet Our Team' page with member cards and contact call-to-action.
app/team/page.tsx.description:
This file defines a Next.js page (app router) that presents a static "Meet Our Team" UI. It exists to provide a public-facing team page that highlights founders and advisors, their photos, short bios, and links to social profiles. The page composes small reusable pieces (Header and Footer components) and uses the Next.js Image component to display circular avatar images. The team member data is defined inline as a JavaScript array of objects and is rendered into a responsive CSS grid using Tailwind utility classes.

The design emphasizes a simple, static presentation: the page is client-rendered as a React component exported as the default from the file. Social icons are embedded inline as SVG React nodes inside each team member's data object which simplifies rendering but couples presentation with data. The component also includes a centered call-to-action area linking to a contact page. This file is a UI-only module and does not make API calls or manage application state beyond static data rendering.

Because it contains both content and inline SVG React nodes, it's both data and presentation; refactoring it to a separate module or to use serializable icon identifiers could help decouple content from view code and improve testability.

components/ui/accordion.tsx.short_description: A small React wrapper around Radix Accordion primitives with Tailwind styling and a chevron icon
components/ui/accordion.tsx.description:
This file provides a thin, opinionated UI component that composes @radix-ui/react-accordion primitives into a project-consistent Accordion component. It exists to centralize the styling and accessibility-safe behavior of accordions across the app so consumers can import a consistent API (Accordion, AccordionItem, AccordionTrigger, AccordionContent) without repeating class names or markup. The file enforces client rendering ("use client") for frameworks like Next.js and uses forwardRef to preserve refs and typed props for each primitive wrapper.

Design decisions: instead of reimplementing accordion logic, the file re-exports and wraps Radix primitives to preserve accessibility and keyboard behavior while layering on Tailwind classes, a ChevronDown icon, a cn utility for class merging, and a couple of displayName assignments for better debugging and devtools. Animation and state-based styling are applied via data-state selectors and Tailwind utility classes; actual animation keyframes are expected to be defined elsewhere in the app's global stylesheet or Tailwind configuration. This pattern makes the component small and maintainable while keeping the accessibility of Radix.

How it fits in the system: this is a UI primitives file used by pages and other components that need accordions (e.g., FAQ pages, settings). Because it imports the project's cn helper and Tailwind classes, changes here often co-change with global CSS/Tailwind config and shared utilities. It also relies on the lucide-react icon set for the chevron — replacing or theming the icon would be done here.

key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes content padding and animation classes to ensure consistent expand/collapse behavior that matches app-wide Tailwind/animation settings.

components/ui/alert.tsx.short_description: Small, accessible Alert UI components with style variants using cva and Tailwind classes
components/ui/alert.tsx.description:
This file defines a small, focused set of presentational React components used to display alert messages in the UI: Alert, AlertTitle, and AlertDescription. It centralizes the markup, accessibility attributes (role="alert"), and styling variants (default and destructive) for alert boxes so callers can consistently render notices, warnings, or error messages across the app. Styling is defined via class-variance-authority (cva) and Tailwind utility classes, and the exported components accept className and variant props to allow localized overrides.

The Alert component is the main wrapper; it applies the role="alert" and composes variant-based classes from alertVariants together with any className passed in. AlertTitle and AlertDescription are small semantic children used to structure the content inside an Alert. The styling includes targeted sibling selectors (e.g., positioning for an inline SVG icon) so icons placed as children will be positioned consistently. The design favors composition and reusability: consumers supply children (e.g., an SVG icon, title, description) and the components handle layout and accessibility.

There are subtle TypeScript typing inconsistencies in the file: AlertTitle and AlertDescription forwardRef generic type parameters don't exactly match the rendered element types (AlertTitle uses HTMLParagraphElement but renders an h5; AlertDescription uses HTMLParagraphElement types but renders a div). This probably compiles in many setups but is an area to clean up to get stricter type-safety. The file depends on a shared utility (cn) for class merging and on project-wide Tailwind styles and conventions. Overall, it's a compact, well-scoped UI primitive intended to be used by other components and pages to render alerts consistently.

key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes styling and variant logic so the Alert wrapper can accept a variant prop and produce consistent CSS classes; modifying alert styling should be done here to affect all Alert usages.

key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a consistent title element with margin/typography classes; consumers use it to keep alert headings visually and semantically consistent.

key_constructs.purpose: Presentational component that renders content (in a div) styled for the alert's descriptive text.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates description styling (text-sm, leading) and allows composition inside Alert containers for clearer layout and spacing.

applies variant-aware styling (default vs destructive) via cva

pitfalls.consequence: Type mismatch warnings or incorrect type inference for refs and props; potential subtle runtime issues when consumers expect specific element methods.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep forwardRef generic types consistent with the actual rendered element (e.g., use HTMLHeadingElement for h5) and run TypeScript strict checks.

pitfalls.prevention: When adding icons, place them as immediate child <svg> siblings of title/description as intended, or update CSS selectors if the DOM structure is changed.

pitfalls.consequence: Reduced accessibility — assistive technologies may not announce urgent messages as intended.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve role="alert" unless there is an explicit accessibility reason to change it, and test with a11y tools or screen readers.

components/ui/aspect-ratio.tsx.short_description: Thin client-side wrapper that re-exports Radix UI's AspectRatio.Root as AspectRatio.
components/ui/aspect-ratio.tsx.description:
This file provides a very small UI abstraction: it re-exports @radix-ui/react-aspect-ratio's Root primitive under the local name AspectRatio and marks the module as a Next.js client component with the "use client" directive. The primary purpose is to centralize the import and naming of the AspectRatio primitive so the rest of the codebase can import a consistent component from the project's components/ui surface rather than depending directly on Radix. This makes it easier to swap implementations, add default props, wrap with additional styling or logic later, or keep imports uniform across the app.

Although the file is minimal, it exists to enforce a single source-of-truth for the AspectRatio component API in the codebase. By exporting AspectRatio (the Root primitive from Radix) from a local file, consumers import from components/ui which improves discoverability and allows future modifications (for example, adding default classNames, types, or additional wrapper logic) without changing all call sites. The "use client" directive enforces client-side rendering semantics required by Radix primitives that interact with the DOM in React 18/Next.js app router environments.

key_constructs.reasoning: Using a local named export centralizes the component API and import path, making it trivial to modify or augment behavior in one place later (for example, to add wrappers, styling, or prop defaults) without touching all consumers.

pitfalls.consequence: Next.js may attempt to render the component on the server, causing runtime errors or hydration mismatches because Radix primitives expect client-side DOM access.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the "use client" directive at the top if the component relies on client-only browser APIs or Radix behavior.

pitfalls.consequence: Loss of the centralized wrapper benefit; harder to change implementations later and inconsistent imports across the codebase.
pitfalls.prevention: Import the AspectRatio from components/ui consistently and update only this file if you need to change the underlying implementation.

pitfalls.consequence: Developers may search this file for other exports and be surprised they're not available; might duplicate code or create inconsistent patterns.
pitfalls.prevention: If more primitives are needed project-wide, explicitly add them to this module so it remains a single source-of-truth.

components/ui/alert-dialog.tsx.short_description: Reusable, styled AlertDialog React components wrapping Radix UI primitives with Tailwind classes.
components/ui/alert-dialog.tsx.description:
This file defines a set of AlertDialog components built on top of @radix-ui/react-alert-dialog primitives and styled for the app with Tailwind utility classes and local helpers (cn, buttonVariants). It exists to provide a consistent, accessible, and themeable alert/dialog API for the rest of the application so consumers don't have to wire up Radix primitives and styles themselves.

The module exports each Radix primitive (Root, Trigger, Portal, Overlay, Content, Title, Description, Action, Cancel) as wrapped components that add default styling, animations, and layout (centered modal, overlay, header/footer formats). ForwardRef is used on several components (Overlay, Content, Title, Description, Action, Cancel) to preserve refs for consumers and keep compatibility with Radix's expectations. The file centralizes animation classes, layout, and button-variant integration so dialogs behave and look consistent across the app.

This file fits into the UI component layer: other pages and components import these exports to show confirmation modals, destructive actions, or any blocking alerts. It depends on local helper modules: cn (class name combiner) from '@/lib/utils' and buttonVariants from '@/components/ui/button'. Because it uses the "use client" directive, these components are client-only and meant to run in the browser (important in Next.js apps).

key_constructs.name: AlertDialog
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level Radix Root primitive exported under a familiar name for use as the dialog container.
key_constructs.reasoning: Keeps the Radix Root primitive available under the app's naming convention so consumers use a consistent API (AlertDialog as the entry point).

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogTrigger
key_constructs.purpose: Radix Trigger primitive re-exported for opening the dialog.
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposes the trigger so callers can attach show behavior while keeping the primitive consistent with the rest of the dialog API.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogPortal
key_constructs.purpose: Radix Portal primitive re-exported to portal dialog content to the document body.
key_constructs.reasoning: Separates portal rendering concerns and keeps Content usage simple and predictable.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogOverlay
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef-wrapped Overlay component that adds default overlay styling and animation classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates the overlay styling/animation in one place so all dialogs share the same backdrop behavior and class merging via cn.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogContent
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef-wrapped Content component that centers the dialog, applies container styling and embeds the Overlay via Portal.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a ready-to-use modal content block with proper layout, animation, and shadow. It also ensures the overlay is rendered alongside the content by wrapping content in the Portal.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogHeader
key_constructs.purpose: Small presentational wrapper for dialog header layout and spacing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Standardizes header layout (centered on small screens, left-align on larger) so dialog titles and descriptions look consistent.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogFooter
key_constructs.purpose: Small presentational wrapper for dialog footer layout that handles button ordering and spacing responsively.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures action and cancel buttons are laid out correctly across breakpoints without consumers needing to manage CSS.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogTitle
key_constructs.purpose: ForwardRef-wrapped Title primitive with default typography classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Adds semantic title styling and forwards refs to maintain accessibility and API parity with Radix primitives.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogDescription
key_constructs.purpose: ForwardRef-wrapped Description primitive with muted text styling.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a standard look for dialog descriptions and keeps the accessibility semantics of Radix components intact.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogAction
key_constructs.purpose: ForwardRef-wrapped Action primitive styled as primary/default button via buttonVariants.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates the action/control style so destructive/confirm actions follow the app's button system.

key_constructs.name: AlertDialogCancel
key_constructs.purpose: ForwardRef-wrapped Cancel primitive styled as an outline button and given responsive margin tweaks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Makes the cancel control follow the button system while ensuring proper spacing against the primary action.

key_behaviors: renders a centered modal dialog with overlay
applies consistent animations and layout for open/close states
exposes Title/Description/Action/Cancel primitives with standardized styling
preserves Radix accessibility/focus behavior via forwarded refs

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the "use client" directive at the top of the file
pitfalls.consequence: On frameworks like Next.js this component may be imported on the server, breaking DOM/ref usage and causing runtime errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the "use client" directive or make a server-compatible wrapper if server rendering is required.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the animation/data-state class names without matching CSS/utility definitions
pitfalls.consequence: Dialog open/close animations or visibility transitions may break or cause flash of unstyled content.
pitfalls.prevention: If changing classes, update the corresponding Tailwind/animation utilities or reference design tokens used across other components.

pitfalls.mistake: Overriding className order incorrectly when merging
pitfalls.consequence: Consumers' className might be unintentionally overridden by defaults (or vice versa), leading to styling inconsistencies.
pitfalls.prevention: Use cn() consistently; follow the established pattern of default classes first and then merging consumer className as done in this file.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming displayName properties are always defined
pitfalls.consequence: Accessing displayName from Radix primitives could be undefined in some builds; relying on them for debugging or tests may be flaky.
pitfalls.prevention: Avoid strict runtime logic that depends on primitive.displayName; displayName here is only used to set a helpful developer label.

key_sections: AlertDialogContent: central layout and Portal/Overlay composition — how overlay and content are rendered together
AlertDialogOverlay: overlay styling and animation classes
AlertDialogAction / AlertDialogCancel: how buttons integrate with the app's buttonVariants
skip_unless_needed: displayName assignments
simple presentational wrappers (AlertDialogHeader, AlertDialogFooter) which only add layout classes
