related_files.reason_to_check: If button variant API or class names change, update AlertDialogAction/Cancel to match.

related_files.relationship: Provides the cn function used to merge classes; any changes to class merging semantics may affect styling here.
related_files.reason_to_check: If cn behavior changes (e.g., handling falsy values differently), ensure overlay and content classes are merged correctly.

related_files.relationship: Radix and Tailwind configuration and client/server rendering settings affect this file's runtime context.
related_files.reason_to_check: If Radix or Tailwind versions/configs change, confirm animations and primitive behavior still match expectations.

components/ui/avatar.tsx.short_description: Client-side React wrapper around Radix Avatar primitives with project styling helpers.
components/ui/avatar.tsx.description:
This file defines small, opinionated React components that wrap @radix-ui/react-avatar primitives to provide a consistent avatar surface across the app. It exists to centralize default sizing, shape, and fallback styling for user avatars so that other parts of the UI can import a single Avatar API rather than remembering CSS classes or Radix details. The file is a client component ("use client") for Next.js and leverages a local className merging utility (cn) to combine consumer classes with the project's default utility classes.

Design-wise it intentionally keeps logic minimal: Avatar is Radix's Root with default fixed classes (relative, rounded-full, h-10, w-10, overflow-hidden), AvatarImage maps directly to the Radix Image primitive and ensures the image fills the round area, and AvatarFallback provides a styled fallback element with center alignment and a muted background. The components are created via React.forwardRef and forward their props to the underlying Radix primitives so they remain flexible for accessibility, event handling, or additional attributes. Display names are set from the Radix primitives to aid debugging in React DevTools.

This file fits in the component/UI layer: it's a small building block for user lists, comment UIs, navbars, etc. It depends on the project's cn utility (for class merging) and the external @radix-ui/react-avatar package. Because it is a thin wrapper, when visual or accessibility changes are needed (size changes, fallback content style, alt attribute conventions), this is the single place to update behaviors app-wide.

key_constructs.name: Avatar
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef component that renders AvatarPrimitive.Root with project default classes (size, shape) and merges incoming className.
key_constructs.reasoning: Use this component to render the avatar container consistently; it centralizes sizing/shape and ensures proper ref forwarding to the Radix root for accessibility and interactions.

key_constructs.name: AvatarImage
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef component that renders AvatarPrimitive.Image and forces image to fill the avatar area (aspect-square, h-full, w-full).
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a single, consistent way to include an image inside the avatar container and ensures images maintain square aspect and correct sizing behavior across the app.

key_constructs.name: AvatarFallback
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef component that renders AvatarPrimitive.Fallback with centered content and muted background for when the image is unavailable.
key_constructs.reasoning: Standardizes fallback content (initials, icon) layout and styling so callers only need to supply the fallback children without reimplementing alignment or background styles.

key_behaviors: renders a rounded avatar container with fixed dimensions
renders an image that fills the avatar area
renders a styled fallback when the image fails to load

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing the cn utility without updating this file
pitfalls.consequence: className merging may break, resulting in lost or duplicated classes and broken avatar styles
pitfalls.prevention: When modifying the cn utility, run UI visual checks and update imports/usages in shared wrapper components like this one.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming AvatarPrimitive.*.displayName is always defined
pitfalls.consequence: Setting displayName from undefined is harmless but may result in less helpful names in React DevTools
pitfalls.prevention: If you want a guaranteed name, assign a string literal (e.g., Avatar.displayName = 'Avatar').

pitfalls.mistake: Overriding styles by passing conflicting className props without using cn
pitfalls.consequence: Consumers may accidentally break sizing/shape constraints (e.g., non-square images or non-rounded containers)
pitfalls.prevention: Encourage consumers to pass classes that augment (not replace) layout-critical styles, or update this wrapper to use a stronger CSS/scoped solution if needed.

key_sections: Avatar: the root container — establishes default size/shape and ref forwarding
AvatarImage: how images are sized and fill the container
AvatarFallback: layout and styling for fallback content
skip_unless_needed: TypeScript generics on forwardRef (boilerplate typings)
displayName assignments (only relevant for devtools/debugging)

tests.example_command: No unit tests target this file specifically. Run the app's frontend test suite (e.g., npm test or pnpm test) or visual/storybook tests if available.

related_files.relationship: Places where Avatar is consumed; these will show typical usage patterns (e.g., passing src or fallback children).
related_files.reason_to_check: If changing avatar dimensions or API, update consumer locations to avoid visual regressions.

related_files.relationship: Contains the cn className merging utility imported as '@/lib/utils'.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to cn semantics affect how additional classes are merged here.

related_files.relationship: Underlying primitive implementation providing Root/Image/Fallback behaviors and accessibility features.
related_files.reason_to_check: If Radix changes API shape (prop names/behavior), this wrapper may need updates.

tests_to_run: Run the frontend/unit test suite if available (e.g., npm test)
Run visual checks or storybook (if present) to confirm avatar appearance

components/ui/button.tsx.short_description: A reusable, styled Button React component with variant and size props using cva and forwardRef.
components/ui/button.tsx.description:
This file defines a single UI primitive: a Button component built on top of Tailwind CSS classes and class-variance-authority (cva). It centralizes the visual variants and sizes for buttons used across the app and exposes a typed React component that consumers can pass variant, size, and standard button attributes to. The component also supports asChild mode (via @radix-ui/react-slot) to allow rendering a different element (e.g., a Link) while retaining the button styling.

The reason this file exists is to standardize button visuals and behaviors across the codebase (padding, sizes, hover states, focus rings, disabled styles, icon spacing rules, etc.). It acts as a small design-system primitive: change the variants here to update all app buttons. It interacts with other parts of the project via the exported buttonVariants (for reuse) and the cn utility (for merging classNames), and relies on global Tailwind configuration and design tokens (color classes like bg-primary) to render correctly. Exporting buttonVariants is useful for composing with other components or for tests/snapshots.

key_constructs.name: buttonVariants
key_constructs.purpose: Defines Tailwind class sets and maps named variants and sizes to classes using class-variance-authority (cva).
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates the variant-size-class mapping in one place so styles are consistent and can be programmatically composed. Exported so other components can reuse or extend the same variants.

key_constructs.name: ButtonProps
key_constructs.purpose: TypeScript interface describing props accepted by the Button component (HTML button attributes + cva variant props + asChild flag).
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides type safety for consumers and ensures variant and size keys match the buttonVariants configuration via VariantProps<typeof buttonVariants>.

key_constructs.name: Button
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef React component that renders either a native button or a Slot (when asChild) with composed classes from buttonVariants and cn.
key_constructs.reasoning: forwardRef allows consumers to get a ref to the underlying element. Using asChild with Slot enables swapping the rendered element while preserving styles (important for routing Link elements). Class composition is handled by buttonVariants and cn so callers only supply semantic props.

key_behaviors: renders a styled button with selectable visual variants
supports multiple sizes including icon
allows rendering another element while preserving styles via asChild

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming ref type will always match the rendered element when asChild is used.
pitfalls.consequence: When asChild is true and a non-button element is rendered (via Slot), the forwarded ref type HTMLButtonElement may be incompatible, causing runtime or TypeScript issues.
pitfalls.prevention: Either narrow/refine types by overloading ButtonProps when asChild is true or document that callers are responsible for correct ref types. Consider using unknown or HTMLElement for the forwarded ref type if needed.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying buttonVariants classes without checking Tailwind config or project utility plugins.
pitfalls.consequence: Some shorthand utility classes (e.g., [&_svg]:size-4) can be from custom plugins — changing them can break icon sizing or require Tailwind config updates.
pitfalls.prevention: Verify Tailwind plugins and tokens used across the project before changing classes. Prefer explicit classes (w-4 h-4) if portability is desired.

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on cva to merge an arbitrary `className` property incorrectly.
pitfalls.consequence: If the shape passed into buttonVariants changes or cva usage differs, className merging may be lost and consumer styles may be overwritten.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the call pattern buttonVariants({ variant, size, className }) consistent and validate that class-variance-authority version supports className merging. Use cn to join classes as done here.

key_sections: buttonVariants: central style definitions for variants and sizes
ButtonProps: how consumers should type props (variant/size/asChild)
Button component body: how classes, ref forwarding, and asChild are implemented
skip_unless_needed: import lines and trivial export line

related_files.relationship: Provides the cn utility used to merge class names; changes there affect how classNames are combined for Button.
related_files.reason_to_check: If cn semantics change (e.g., argument order or falsy handling), Button's class composition and conditional styling may break.

related_files.relationship: Contains utility and plugin configuration (custom colors, size utilities) the button variants rely on.
related_files.reason_to_check: Button uses custom token classes (bg-primary, text-primary-foreground) and possibly utility plugins (size-*) that must be present in Tailwind config.

related_files.relationship: Icon components used inside Button must respect the CSS selectors (e.g., [&_svg]:size-4) for consistent sizing.
related_files.reason_to_check: If icons change their internal markup (e.g., not using <svg>), the button's icon sizing rules may no longer apply.

components/ui/badge.tsx.short_description: A small React Badge UI component with variant styling powered by class-variance-authority and Tailwind classes.
components/ui/badge.tsx.description:
This file defines a reusable Badge UI component for the application’s design system. It centralizes badge styling and variants (default, secondary, destructive, outline) using class-variance-authority (cva) to produce deterministic className strings and exposes both the Badge React component and the badgeVariants helper for other code to reuse. The component is a thin wrapper around a div element that merges generated variant classes with any custom className via a local cn utility.

The reason this file exists is to provide a single, typed source of truth for badge visuals and interactions so consumers across the app use consistent styles. It uses VariantProps<typeof badgeVariants> to infer allowed variant prop values at compile time, which helps maintain type-safe usage across the codebase. The CSS class string includes Tailwind utility classes (bg-primary, text-primary-foreground, focus:ring, etc.), implying it is part of a Tailwind-based design system with custom tokens.

Design decisions to call out: cva is used to manage variants and defaultVariants, keeping variant logic declarative and composable; exporting badgeVariants allows other components or tests to compute the same class string without rendering Badge; BadgeProps extends React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement] so arbitrary div props (including event handlers) can be forwarded. Note a UX/semantic caveat: the element is a div and includes focus styles in CSS, but a div is not keyboard-focusable by default (so focus styles won't apply without tabIndex or a focusable role).

key_constructs.name: badgeVariants
key_constructs.purpose: Defines variant-aware Tailwind class strings using class-variance-authority (cva).
key_constructs.reasoning: Use cva so variants are centrally defined, typed, and reusable. Agents modifying styles should update this constant to change the visual spec across all badges. Exported so other components can compute the same class output.

key_constructs.name: BadgeProps
key_constructs.purpose: TypeScript interface combining standard div attributes with cva VariantProps for badgeVariants.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides type-safe contract for the Badge component: consumers get autocompletion and compile-time checks for allowed variants and HTML attributes.

key_constructs.name: Badge
key_constructs.purpose: React functional component that renders a div with merged variant and custom classes and forwards remaining props.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the component entrypoint used throughout the UI. When updating behavior (e.g., making the badge keyboard-focusable or a different element), change this function. It’s intentionally minimal to keep styling and semantics orthogonal to usage.

key_behaviors: renders a styled badge element
applies variant-specific classes
merges custom className and forwards HTML div props

pitfalls.mistake: Treating the Badge as keyboard-focusable because focus styles exist
pitfalls.consequence: Users using keyboard navigation won't be able to focus the badge (accessibility issue) and focus styles won't be visible by default.
pitfalls.prevention: If the badge should be focusable, change to a focusable element (button/a) or add tabIndex/role and keyboard handlers; otherwise remove focus styles.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying class strings inline rather than updating badgeVariants
pitfalls.consequence: Inconsistent badge visuals across the app and duplication of variant logic.
pitfalls.prevention: Update badgeVariants so all consumers benefit from changes and types remain accurate.

pitfalls.mistake: Passing an invalid variant string at runtime (bypassing TS)
pitfalls.consequence: cva will still generate a string, but it may not match any intended CSS token, leading to unexpected appearance.
pitfalls.prevention: Prefer TypeScript-checked usage and/or validate props at runtime if values can come from untyped sources.

key_sections: badgeVariants: variant definitions and defaultVariants; change this to alter visuals
BadgeProps: shows the typing for allowed props including VariantProps<typeof badgeVariants>
Badge: how className merging and prop forwarding are done
skip_unless_needed: the large Tailwind class string in cva’s base argument (boilerplate unless adjusting styles)

related_files.relationship: Provides cn utility used to merge className strings for this component.
related_files.reason_to_check: If cn's behavior changes (e.g., deduplication), badge class merging may be affected; also check when updating how class names are combined or filtered.

related_files.relationship: Defines Tailwind tokens (bg-primary, text-primary-foreground, etc.) referenced by the badge classes.
related_files.reason_to_check: If design tokens or color names change, update badgeVariants accordingly to keep visual consistency.

related_files.relationship: Other UI components in the same directory form the shared design system and often co-change with Badge.
related_files.reason_to_check: When updating global spacing, border-radius, or focus styles, check other components for consistency.

yarn typecheck
run storybook or visual regression tests if available

components/ui/calendar.tsx.short_description: A Next.js client React wrapper around react-day-picker that provides Tailwind-styled calendar UI.
components/ui/calendar.tsx.description:
This file defines a client-side React component named Calendar that wraps the DayPicker component from the react-day-picker library and applies project-specific styling and behavior. It centralizes how calendars are rendered in the app by mapping DayPicker's classNames to Tailwind utility classes and by providing consistent navigation buttons (using lucide-react icons) and variant button styles (via buttonVariants). The component forwards all DayPicker props and merges incoming className/classNames with its defaults, making it easy to use the same visual style across pages and components.

It exists to provide a single, opinionated calendar component consistent with the app's design system (buttonVariants and cn utilities). The file is a 'use client' component (Next.js App Router) which means it intentionally runs on the client and can rely on client-only behavior (event handlers, DOM interactions). The design decision to wrap DayPicker rather than reimplementing calendar logic keeps the code small and leverages a maintained calendar library for correctness while centralizing styling and icons.

Key behaviors: the component sets a default showOutsideDays prop to true, applies a set of classNames that shape layout, spacing, and state styles (selected, disabled, today, range-related classes), and replaces the navigation icons with ChevronLeft/ChevronRight. It also places most classNames inline, but respects an incoming classNames prop by spreading it last (so callers can override defaults). The file is small and focused on presentation rather than date logic; it delegates date handling and selection behavior to DayPicker via forwarded props.

key_constructs.name: CalendarProps
key_constructs.purpose: A type alias for the props accepted by the Calendar component, equal to React.ComponentProps<typeof DayPicker> so the wrapper accepts the same props as DayPicker.
key_constructs.reasoning: Using CalendarProps ensures the wrapper stays type-compatible with react-day-picker and automatically inherits prop definitions and changes from the upstream DayPicker type, reducing maintenance burden and improving DX for editors/TS consumers.

key_constructs.name: Calendar
key_constructs.purpose: The main exported React component that wraps DayPicker, applying default props, merged classNames, and custom icons.
key_constructs.reasoning: Calendar centralizes visual and interaction conventions for all calendar use in the app. It merges incoming className and classNames props with a curated set of Tailwind classes and buttonVariants to produce consistent styling, and forwards all other DayPicker props unmodified so consumers can control selection, locale, mode (single/range), etc.

key_behaviors: renders a styled calendar UI using react-day-picker
forwards DayPicker props while applying design-system classNames
provides custom navigation icons and button styles

pitfalls.mistake: Mutating or expecting classNames order to be irrelevant when overriding defaults.
pitfalls.consequence: If you supply classNames without knowing the merge order, you might unintentionally override or fail to override specific sub-classes (the spread places incoming classNames last to let callers override defaults).
pitfalls.prevention: Use the same className keys as the defaults (e.g., 'day', 'month', 'nav_button') and test the component visually; rely on the classNames spread ordering when designing overrides.

pitfalls.mistake: Using this component on the server (SSR) or forgetting it is a client component.
pitfalls.consequence: Next.js will throw an error or produce hydration mismatches if you attempt to render this as a server component because it includes 'use client'.
pitfalls.prevention: Only import Calendar from client components or wrap usage in a client boundary; keep event handling and DOM-only features within client code.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming react-day-picker behavior or types never change.
pitfalls.consequence: If react-day-picker changes its props or classNames API, CalendarProps and the classNames mapping might become incorrect, causing type errors or broken styling.
pitfalls.prevention: When upgrading react-day-picker, validate types and visually smoke-test calendar variants (single, range, disabled days). Keep CalendarProps tied to DayPicker via the type alias to catch some type-level changes.

key_sections: classNames mapping: defines the Tailwind classes applied to DayPicker parts (months, month, caption, nav, table, head_cell, cell, day, day_selected, day_disabled, etc.)
props handling: default showOutsideDays, merging of className and classNames, and props forwarding
skip_unless_needed: icons components: IconLeft/IconRight small inline components (trivial to change)
buttonVariants call details if you are just changing layout

related_files.relationship: This component wraps DayPicker from this library; check its docs/types when changing CalendarProps or classNames.
related_files.reason_to_check: Upgrading react-day-picker or changing its className API will require updates here.

related_files.relationship: Provides buttonVariants used to style day and nav buttons; changes to buttonVariants affect calendar button styles.
related_files.reason_to_check: If button variants change (classNames or API), the calendar's button sizing/appearance may need adjustment.

related_files.relationship: Provides cn (className join utility) used to merge className strings.
related_files.reason_to_check: If cn behavior changes (e.g., different falsy handling), class merging semantics could be affected.

components/ui/breadcrumb.tsx.short_description: Accessible React breadcrumb UI primitives (Breadcrumb, list, items, links, separators, ellipsis).
components/ui/breadcrumb.tsx.description:
This file provides a small set of reusable, accessible breadcrumb components implemented as React forwardRef components in TypeScript. The primitives are designed to be composed: a top-level Breadcrumb (nav) wraps a BreadcrumbList (ol) of BreadcrumbItem (li), which contain BreadcrumbLink (a or custom child), BreadcrumbPage (current page span), BreadcrumbSeparator (separator li) and BreadcrumbEllipsis (compact 'more' indicator). The components centralize styling (via cn utility) and accessibility attributes (aria-label, aria-current, role, aria-disabled), so consumers can build breadcrumb UIs consistently across the app.

Design decisions: components are minimal and composable rather than providing a single all-in-one Breadcrumb renderer. This supports flexibility for different markup or routing strategies (e.g., custom Link components via asChild). Forwarding refs for each primitive enables integration with other libraries or tests. The file intentionally uses semantic HTML elements (nav, ol, li, a, span) and accessibility roles/attributes so breadcrumbs are screen-reader friendly. It relies on external small utilities and icon libraries (cn, lucide-react icons, radix Slot) rather than reimplementing them.

How it fits in the system: these are UI primitives meant to be imported and composed in page/Header components or layout templates that need breadcrumb navigation. Because BreadcrumbLink supports asChild, it interoperates with router link components (Next.js Link, React Router Link) by allowing consumers to pass their link as the child. The file focuses only on presentation and semantics — it does not perform routing or compute breadcrumb items; that logic belongs to higher-level code that uses these primitives.

key_constructs.name: Breadcrumb
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level nav wrapper for breadcrumb content with aria-label.
key_constructs.reasoning: Acts as the semantic nav container and exposes a place to attach refs and attributes; the type includes an optional separator prop in its signature even though the implementation merely spreads props, so consumers can pass but the prop is not used by this wrapper.

key_constructs.name: BreadcrumbList
key_constructs.purpose: Ordered list (ol) that lays out breadcrumb items with spacing and text styles.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates consistent styling and structure for the list of breadcrumb items including responsive gaps and truncation behavior, so pages don't duplicate these visual rules.

key_constructs.name: BreadcrumbItem
key_constructs.purpose: List item (li) wrapper for individual breadcrumb entries.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides inline-flex alignment and spacing for link/separator pairs; keeps item-level layout centralized.

key_constructs.name: BreadcrumbLink
key_constructs.purpose: Anchor-like component that can render either an <a> or a custom child via radix Slot (asChild).
key_constructs.reasoning: Supports composition with routing libraries: asChild lets consumers pass a router Link component which receives the className and ref, avoiding the need to reimplement styling for each router's Link.

key_constructs.name: BreadcrumbPage
key_constructs.purpose: Span representing the current page with aria-current and aria-disabled semantics.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides the accessible pattern for current breadcrumb item (non-link) with the correct aria attributes and consistent styling.

key_constructs.name: BreadcrumbSeparator
key_constructs.purpose: Separator element (li) typically rendering a chevron icon between breadcrumb items.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the separator markup (icon, size classes and role/aria-hidden) so separators are consistent and accessible by default; also accepts children to override icon.

key_constructs.name: BreadcrumbEllipsis
key_constructs.purpose: Compact marker (span) that indicates collapsed items (a 'more' control) using a horizontal dots icon.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a visual affordance for collapsed breadcrumb sections; includes sr-only text ('More') for screen-reader clarity.

key_behaviors: renders an accessible <nav> with aria-label="breadcrumb"
provides an ordered list layout for breadcrumb items
supports rendering links or custom link components via asChild
renders an accessible current-page span with aria-current
renders default separator icon (ChevronRight) and ellipsis (MoreHorizontal)

pitfalls.mistake: Assume Breadcrumb component uses its declared separator prop.
pitfalls.consequence: Passing separator to Breadcrumb has no effect because the prop is only declared in the type signature and not used in the component implementation.
pitfalls.prevention: Pass separators explicitly as children (BreadcrumbSeparator) or modify Breadcrumb implementation to consume the separator prop if intended.

pitfalls.mistake: Rely on BreadcrumbEllipsis.displayName value for debugging or tests.
pitfalls.consequence: displayName is misspelled as "BreadcrumbElipssis" which may confuse component tree inspection or snapshot utilities.
pitfalls.prevention: Correct the displayName string to "BreadcrumbEllipsis" or avoid depending on displayName in tests.

pitfalls.mistake: Expect refs on BreadcrumbSeparator and BreadcrumbEllipsis (they don't forward refs).
pitfalls.consequence: Attempting to attach a ref will not receive the DOM node for these functional wrappers.
pitfalls.prevention: If refs are needed, update these components to forwardRef similarly to other primitives.

key_sections: Breadcrumb: the outer nav wrapper and its accessible label

components/ui/card.tsx.short_description: A small set of reusable, styled Card components (container + header/title/description/content/footer) for the UI library.
components/ui/card.tsx.description:
This file defines a compact design-system style Card component plus a set of semantic subcomponents (CardHeader, CardTitle, CardDescription, CardContent, CardFooter). Each export is a React.forwardRef wrapper around a div with a set of Tailwind CSS utility classes applied and the ability to merge additional className props via the imported cn helper. The purpose is to provide a consistent, reusable building block for card-like UI elements across the application, encapsulating layout/spacing/typography so pages and other components can compose cards without duplicating styles.

Design decisions: each piece is implemented as a simple forwardRef functional component typed to HTMLDivElement and React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement>, which allows consumers to pass any standard div props and obtain a ref to the DOM node. The default classes encode theme tokens (e.g., bg-card, text-card-foreground) and spacing conventions, so the components act as opinionated primitives that can be customized through className merging. displayName is set on each component to aid debugging and tools that rely on component names. The file intentionally keeps logic minimal — no state or event handling — focusing on consistent markup and styling.

How it fits: This is a presentational UI primitives file likely consumed by pages, feature components, and Storybook examples. It is a small piece of a larger component library or design system within the repo. Changes here affect many UIs that use cards, so this file often co-changes with global styling/tailwind theme config, a top-level components index/barrel export, and the cn utility used to merge class names.

key_constructs.name: Card
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level card container component that renders a div with base card styles and merges provided className and props; forwards ref to the div.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the main export consumers will use to create card containers. It centralizes the base classes (rounded border, background, text color, shadow) so that layout consistency is preserved across the app. Forwarding ref and accepting HTMLAttributes keeps it flexible for integration with tooltips, focus management, and testing.

key_constructs.name: CardHeader
key_constructs.purpose: Provides a styled header region inside a Card with spacing and vertical layout (flex-col) for title/description.
key_constructs.reasoning: Separating header markup allows consistent spacing and alignment for title/metadata and avoids duplicating classes in consumers.

key_constructs.purpose: Renders the title region of a card with typography utilities (font size, weight, tracking) and forwards refs/props.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates title styling so consumers only need to provide content, ensuring typography consistency.

key_constructs.name: CardDescription
key_constructs.purpose: Renders a smaller, muted-foreground description block inside a card.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a single place to tune description sizing and muted color token usage without impacting consumers.

key_constructs.name: CardContent
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapper for the main content area of a card, applies default padding and removes top padding to stack with headers.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates content spacing conventions and makes it easy to drop children into a correctly padded region.

key_constructs.name: CardFooter
key_constructs.purpose: Footer region for actions or metadata inside a card; aligns items and applies padding similar to content but with flex layout.
key_constructs.reasoning: Standardizes where actions/buttons live and their spacing relative to content.

exposes semantic subcomponents for header/title/description/content/footer

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the order of className merging so consumer classes override defaults unintentionally or vice versa.
pitfalls.consequence: Visual regression where defaults (rounded corners, background, shadows) are overwritten or impossible to override.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the existing cn(classDefaults, className) pattern or clearly document expected override semantics; use more specific utility classes if necessary.

pitfalls.mistake: Replacing divs with semantic elements (e.g., header, footer) without auditing CSS and accessibility implications.
pitfalls.consequence: Could improve semantics but also break layout assumptions or CSS selectors; tests and styling may need updates.
pitfalls.prevention: If changing tags, update any selectors and run UI/story tests; consider adding role attributes instead of changing element types in a breaking way.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing forwardRef or changing typing from HTMLDivElement to a different type.
pitfalls.consequence: Consumers that rely on refs (focus, measurements) may break; TypeScript errors may appear in callers.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep forwardRef with proper typing or provide a migration path and bump package major if API changes are breaking.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming cn is available or behaves like classnames; using undefined/incorrect implementation.
pitfalls.consequence: className merges may output unexpected strings, causing broken styles or runtime exceptions.
pitfalls.prevention: Check and test the cn helper in '@/lib/utils' and ensure consistent behavior (handles falsy values, arrays, objects) across the codebase.

components/ui/checkbox.tsx.short_description: A styled, accessible Checkbox React component wrapper around Radix UI with an icon indicator.
components/ui/checkbox.tsx.description:
This file defines a single reusable Checkbox React component that adapts @radix-ui/react-checkbox to the project's design system. It exists to centralize styling, accessibility behaviors, and the visual indicator (a Check icon from lucide-react) so other parts of the app can import a consistent Checkbox UI. The "use client" directive indicates this component is a client-side Next.js component (stateful/interactive), appropriate because it forwards refs and relies on DOM attributes from Radix.

The component uses React.forwardRef to expose the underlying Radix Root element ref to callers, and types the props with ComponentPropsWithoutRef<typeof CheckboxPrimitive.Root> so it accepts the full Radix Checkbox primitive API (checked, defaultChecked, onCheckedChange, disabled, etc.). Styling is applied by composing a large set of Tailwind/CSS utility classes through the local cn utility to merge any passed className with the default classes. Visual states (checked, disabled, focus) are controlled via Radix's data-state attribute selectors (data-[state=checked]) and standard focus-visible classes. The indicator is always a lucide-react Check icon rendered inside CheckboxPrimitive.Indicator.

Design decisions: wrapping Radix lets the app rely on a consistent style and behavior across checkboxes while still exposing the primitive's props. The component sets Checkbox.displayName to CheckboxPrimitive.Root.displayName to aid in DevTools/React debugging; however, that relies on the imported primitive's displayName being defined. The implementation intentionally keeps the indicator simple (always a check) and doesn't differentiate an indeterminate state visually (Radix supports it but the UI here will still render the check icon unless additional logic is added).

key_constructs.name: Checkbox
key_constructs.purpose: The exported React component that renders a styled Radix Checkbox with a Check icon indicator and forwards refs.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the main public API in the file. It centralizes styling and behavior for checkboxes so other components can import a single, opinionated Checkbox that accepts all Radix props and forwards refs for form integrations and focus management.

key_constructs.name: cn
key_constructs.purpose: Utility used to compose and merge className strings (imported from '@/lib/utils').
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to merge default style classes with any className passed in via props. When editing styles or adding classes, changes should be applied through cn to preserve user-supplied classNames.

key_constructs.name: CheckboxPrimitive.Root
key_constructs.purpose: The underlying Radix primitive component used as the actual checkbox input root.
key_constructs.reasoning: Understanding that Checkbox simply wraps this primitive helps when debugging props/state flow (e.g., data-state attributes, indeterminate support) and when needing to pass or type additional Radix-specific props.

key_behaviors: renders a styled checkbox with an icon indicator
forwards refs to the Radix Checkbox root element
accepts and forwards all Radix Checkbox props (checked, disabled, onCheckedChange)
applies visual states using Radix data-state attributes and CSS utility classes

pitfalls.mistake: Assigning Checkbox.displayName to CheckboxPrimitive.Root.displayName assuming it is defined.
pitfalls.consequence: If CheckboxPrimitive.Root.displayName is undefined, the component will end up with an undefined displayName which may reduce devtools readability; not a runtime error but hampers debugging.
pitfalls.prevention: Set an explicit string (e.g., 'Checkbox') or fallback to a literal if the imported displayName is undefined.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming indeterminate state has a visual representation.
pitfalls.consequence: When Radix sets data-state='indeterminate', this component will still render the Check icon (it doesn't handle indeterminate separately), causing a mismatch between state semantics and visual cue.
pitfalls.prevention: Add conditional rendering inside CheckboxPrimitive.Indicator to show a different icon/mark for indeterminate or observe data-state attribute.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the large className string without awareness of dependent CSS tokens (e.g., 'primary', 'ring').
pitfalls.consequence: Visual regressions across the app where other components expect the same tokens/classes; unexpected color or focus behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Update global design tokens/CSS variables and run visual/manual tests (storybook/screenshots) to validate changes.

key_sections: Checkbox: the forwardRef-wrapped component — entry point and public API
className string in CheckboxPrimitive.Root: defines all styling and state-dependent classes
CheckboxPrimitive.Indicator: renders the visual check icon
skip_unless_needed: imports block (boilerplate), except review cn and Radix imports when debugging behavior

related_files.reason_to_check: If styling/memoization/conditional class merging behavior changes in cn, verify this Checkbox still applies classes as expected.

related_files.reason_to_check: When changing visual tokens or conventions (focus rings, disabled opacity), update sibling primitives for consistency.

components/ui/carousel.tsx.short_description: React Embla-based accessible Carousel wrapper with context and controls
components/ui/carousel.tsx.description:
This file implements a reusable Carousel UI component built on top of embla-carousel-react. It provides a high-level Carousel component that initializes the Embla carousel, exposes its API via context to child components, and supplies several purpose-built subcomponents (CarouselContent, CarouselItem, CarouselPrevious, CarouselNext) that consume that context. The file exists to encapsulate carousel initialization, state (canScrollPrev/canScrollNext), keyboard handling, and control rendering, so other parts of the app can compose accessible carousels without dealing with embla's lifecycle and DOM wiring directly.

Design-wise, the Carousel component wraps useEmblaCarousel and stores both the ref function (carouselRef) and the api returned by Embla in context. Children access the context via useCarousel, so layout components (CarouselContent/CarouselItem) only need to apply layout classes and the Embla ref, while control components (CarouselPrevious/CarouselNext) call scrollPrev/scrollNext and disable themselves based on canScrollPrev/canScrollNext. There are several accessibility-minded additions: the container has role="region" and aria-roledescription="carousel", items are role="group" with aria-roledescription="slide", and the action buttons include sr-only text. The component also attempts keyboard navigation via ArrowLeft/ArrowRight and provides a hook to expose the underlying Embla API via an optional setApi prop.

key_constructs.name: Carousel
key_constructs.purpose: Top-level forwardRef React component that initializes Embla, manages scroll availability state, provides context to children, and handles keyboard navigation.
key_constructs.reasoning: Start here to understand Embla initialization (useEmblaCarousel), how the api and carouselRef are propagated, and how children are expected to consume the carousel context. Changes to lifecycle, event wiring, or keyboard behavior belong here.

key_constructs.name: CarouselContent
key_constructs.purpose: Child component that receives the embla ref and renders the scrolling container and an inner flex layout used for slides.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is where the embla ref is attached and layout (horizontal vs vertical) classes are applied. If slides are not being recognized by Embla, inspect this component for ref wiring and classNames.

key_constructs.name: CarouselItem
key_constructs.purpose: Slide wrapper component that applies slide-specific classes, spacing, and ARIA roles for each slide.
key_constructs.reasoning: Use this to wrap each slide's content. It sets role and spacing so slides behave correctly in the flex layout and are recognized by embla as items.

key_constructs.name: CarouselPrevious
key_constructs.purpose: A button component to navigate to the previous slide; consumes context to call scrollPrev and to disable itself when can't scroll prev.
key_constructs.reasoning: Modify or style this when changing control appearance/placement or when changing how navigation permission is derived.

key_constructs.name: CarouselNext
key_constructs.purpose: A button component to navigate to the next slide; consumes context to call scrollNext and to disable itself when can't scroll next.
key_constructs.reasoning: Symmetric to CarouselPrevious; check this when debugging forward navigation or disabled state logic.

key_constructs.name: CarouselContext
key_constructs.purpose: React context that carries carouselRef, api, scrollPrev/Next functions, canScrollPrev/Next flags, and original props like orientation.
key_constructs.reasoning: All carousel subcomponents consume this context. If a child fails to get the carouselRef or API, first ensure this provider is present and that useCarousel is used inside the provider.

key_constructs.name: useCarousel
key_constructs.purpose: Custom hook that reads CarouselContext and throws if used outside a Carousel provider.
key_constructs.reasoning: Use this in any child that needs carousel internals; it guards against out-of-tree usage and surfaces a clear error message.

key_constructs.name: CarouselApi
key_constructs.purpose: Type alias for the Embla API type (exported to allow consumers to type annotate setApi handlers).
key_constructs.reasoning: Useful for TS users who want to receive the embla api through setApi and interact with it programmatically.

key_behaviors: initializes the Embla carousel and attaches the carousel ref
provides a context-based API to child components
enables previous/next navigation buttons and disables them appropriately
supports keyboard navigation with left/right arrow keys
optionally exposes the embla api to parent via setApi prop

pitfalls.mistake: Binding onSelect directly to embla events (api.on('select', onSelect)) while onSelect expects the api argument.
pitfalls.consequence: If embla invokes the listener without passing the api instance, onSelect will receive undefined and return early — subsequent select events won't update canScrollPrev/canScrollNext, causing controls to remain stale.
pitfalls.prevention: Bind handlers that capture the api via closure: api.on('select', () => onSelect(api)) or change onSelect to read the api from closure rather than expecting it as an argument.

pitfalls.mistake: Not removing all event listeners in cleanup (the code registers both 'reInit' and 'select' but only removes 'select').
pitfalls.consequence: Potential memory leaks or duplicate event handling if the component unmounts/reinitializes repeatedly.
pitfalls.prevention: Call api.off for every event you register (off('select') and off('reInit')), or keep references to handler wrappers and remove them precisely.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming left/right arrow keys are appropriate for all orientations.
pitfalls.consequence: Vertical carousels won't respond to ArrowUp/ArrowDown; keyboard accessibility will be degraded for vertical orientation.
pitfalls.prevention: Handle ArrowUp/ArrowDown when orientation is vertical, or map keys appropriately based on orientation.

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on the explicit orientation prop even when opts.axis is set.
pitfalls.consequence: opts.axis may be ignored if orientation has a default; you may get inconsistent orientation if both are provided.
pitfalls.prevention: Either prioritize opts.axis when provided or avoid setting a default orientation when opts.axis should determine orientation.

key_sections: Carousel: how useEmblaCarousel is invoked, how api and carouselRef are stored in context, how onSelect is wired
useCarousel: how children obtain context and the guard that enforces correct usage
CarouselContent/CarouselItem: how the embla ref is attached and the layout classes applied
CarouselPrevious/CarouselNext: how controls consume context, call scrollPrev/scrollNext, and set disabled state
skip_unless_needed: Type alias declarations at the top (useful only for TypeScript typing changes)
Minor className utility (cn) usage details

related_files.reason_to_check: If button props like size/variant change or Button's ref forwarding is modified, update CarouselPrevious/Next accordingly.

related_files.relationship: Provides cn utility used for merging className strings.
related_files.reason_to_check: If classNames handling changes (e.g., API of cn), Carousel's class merging may need to be adjusted.

related_files.relationship: Relies on embla-carousel-react API (useEmblaCarousel return shape and event semantics).
related_files.reason_to_check: If embla's event listener signatures or return types change, Carousel event wiring must be updated.

components/ui/chart.tsx.short_description: A small React wrapper around Recharts that provides theming, shared config, and custom tooltip/legend UI.
components/ui/chart.tsx.description:
This file provides a set of React components and helpers that standardize how charts (using the Recharts library) are styled and displayed across the app. Its main purpose is to centralize theme-aware color configuration for chart series, provide a Context to share that configuration with chart subcomponents, and to offer custom Tooltip and Legend content components that read the shared configuration to present consistent labels, icons, and indicator colors. It exists to encapsulate presentation concerns (colors, tooltips, legends, layout) so that each chart instance only needs to supply data and a small config object describing series metadata.

Design-wise, the module wraps Recharts' ResponsiveContainer and some primitives (Tooltip, Legend) with components that integrate a ChartConfig type. ChartConfig allows each series key to define a label, an icon component, and either a single color (color) or a theme-aware color mapping (theme). The ChartContainer produces a small, unique data-chart attribute and emits a <style> element (via dangerouslySetInnerHTML) that defines CSS custom properties --color-{key} scoped to the chart and optionally to a .dark selector (to support Tailwind-style dark mode). Tooltip and Legend content components read the ChartConfig from context and use it to show custom labels/icons and to set indicator colors. Helper getPayloadConfigFromPayload extracts the appropriate config entry for a tooltip/legend payload item, using some fallback heuristics to support different payload shapes from Recharts.

This file is a focused presentation layer: it doesn't manage chart data or business logic; instead it normalizes styling and UX for charts. It expects Recharts primitives to be used as children of ChartContainer (within ResponsiveContainer). Because it injects CSS based on strings coming from configuration, it centralizes theme mapping and makes color changes for a chart instance simple and local to that instance.

key_constructs.name: THEMES
key_constructs.purpose: Defines mapping of theme keys to CSS selector prefixes used when emitting per-chart style rules (supports light and Tailwind-like .dark).
key_constructs.reasoning: Keeping theme selector mapping here provides a single place to add more theme variants and is used by ChartStyle to emit theme-scoped CSS variables.

key_constructs.name: ChartConfig
key_constructs.purpose: Type alias describing per-series metadata (label, icon) and either a single color or a per-theme color mapping.
key_constructs.reasoning: This type drives how colors/labels/icons are configured for charts. Understanding it is key to adding new series or modifying theme colors.

key_constructs.name: ChartContext
key_constructs.purpose: React context that holds the chart configuration object so children (tooltip/legend) can access it without prop drilling.
key_constructs.reasoning: Using context decouples the tooltip/legend components from the ChartContainer's props while ensuring they all use the same config.

key_constructs.name: useChart
key_constructs.purpose: Custom hook that returns ChartContext and throws a helpful error when used outside ChartContainer.
key_constructs.reasoning: Enforces that tooltip/legend components are used only within the ChartContainer so they reliably access config.

key_constructs.name: ChartContainer
key_constructs.purpose: Main wrapper component: provides ChartContext, renders a data-chart attribute, injects ChartStyle, and wraps children in Recharts' ResponsiveContainer.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes layout, unique scoping of CSS variables, and context provision for chart instances.

key_constructs.name: ChartStyle
key_constructs.purpose: Generates a style tag (dangerouslySetInnerHTML) that defines --color-{key} CSS variables scoped to the chart's data-chart selector and optionally to themes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Emits per-instance CSS variables to allow chart elements (and the tooltip/legend) to reference consistent colors without inlining styles on many elements.

key_constructs.name: ChartTooltip
key_constructs.purpose: Re-exports Recharts' Tooltip component for convenience.
key_constructs.reasoning: Small convenience alias so consumers can import the Tooltip from this module.

key_constructs.name: ChartTooltipContent
key_constructs.purpose: Custom tooltip renderer that reads payload entries, uses ChartConfig to show labels/icons, formats values, and renders indicator shapes (dot/line/dashed).
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides consistent tooltip UI across charts and supports features like hiding labels/indicators, custom label formatting, and theme-aware indicator colors.

key_constructs.name: ChartLegend
key_constructs.purpose: Re-exports Recharts' Legend component for convenience.
key_constructs.reasoning: Convenience alias similar to ChartTooltip.

key_constructs.name: ChartLegendContent
key_constructs.purpose: Custom legend renderer that maps Recharts' payload to configured icons/labels and a small color swatch.
key_constructs.reasoning: Keeps legend presentation consistent and allows use of configured icons and labels.

key_constructs.name: getPayloadConfigFromPayload
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that finds the appropriate ChartConfig entry for a Recharts payload item by looking at payload, payload.payload and falling back to provided key.
key_constructs.reasoning: Recharts payload objects vary in shape; this function centralizes heuristics to resolve the config entry (which supplies labels/icons/colors) reliably.

key_behaviors: provides theme-aware CSS variables for chart series
shares series config via React context
renders custom tooltip content using series config
renders custom legend content using series config

pitfalls.mistake: Place untrusted or unsanitized strings into ChartConfig.theme/color values and assume they are safe.
pitfalls.consequence: Because ChartStyle injects a style tag via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, malicious values could break CSS or introduce injection risks.
pitfalls.prevention: Only pass trusted color strings (e.g., hex/RGB/CSS variables) or validate/sanitize config values before using them in ChartConfig.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume numeric 0 will show up in tooltip values.
pitfalls.consequence: The code uses `item.value && ...` to decide whether to render the formatted numeric value; 0 is falsy and will not be displayed, losing correct data presentation.
pitfalls.prevention: Change the existence check to `item.value !== undefined && item.value !== null` or similar truthy test that allows 0.

pitfalls.mistake: Rely on payload keys always being strings matching config keys.
pitfalls.consequence: getPayloadConfigFromPayload uses heuristics to determine a config key (checking payload and payload.payload); if payload shape differs or keys don't match, wrong config or undefined may be returned leading to missing labels/icons.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure chart data's dataKey/name fields match configured keys, or adjust getPayloadConfigFromPayload to more robustly map keys (or log mismatches during development).

pitfalls.mistake: Assume ChartLegendContent item.value is unique for React keys.
pitfalls.consequence: The legend uses item.value as key which may not be unique and can lead to React key collisions or incorrect updating.
pitfalls.prevention: Use a more stable unique key (e.g., dataKey or combined index) when rendering legend items.

key_sections: ChartContainer: central wrapper and context provider—understand how config is passed and how the data-chart id is generated
ChartStyle: how per-chart CSS variables are emitted and how THEMES are used
ChartTooltipContent: tooltip rendering logic and use of getPayloadConfigFromPayload
getPayloadConfigFromPayload: heuristics for mapping Recharts payloads to ChartConfig entries
skip_unless_needed: THEMES constant (small mapping) unless you need to add theme variants
RechartsPrimitive aliases (ChartTooltip, ChartLegend) which are trivial re-exports

related_files.path: app/(charts)/* or other components that render charts (not provided)
related_files.relationship: Files that actually instantiate ChartContainer and pass data/config; these will use the config shape and are natural co-change candidates when changing ChartConfig or Tooltip/Legend behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: If updating ChartConfig shape, THEMES, or getPayloadConfigFromPayload heuristics, all chart-using components should be updated and verified.

related_files.relationship: Provides the `cn` utility used by this module (className merging).
related_files.reason_to_check: If `cn` behavior changes (e.g., different class merging), it can affect styling classes emitted here.

tests_to_run: Run the app's UI tests or storybook entries that display charts (no unit tests included in repo for this file).
If adding unit tests: run jest/your test runner for chart-related specs.

components/ui/collapsible.tsx.short_description: Thin client-side re-export of Radix Collapsible primitives for app-wide use
components/ui/collapsible.tsx.description:
This file is a minimal adapter that re-exports Radix UI's Collapsible primitives (Root, CollapsibleTrigger, CollapsibleContent) as named exports for the application's UI component layer. The top-level "use client" directive marks this module as a client component in Next.js (or any environment that supports React server/client separation), which is required because Radix primitives are interactive and depend on client-side behavior.

The purpose of centralizing these re-exports is practical: it gives the codebase a single import path for collapsible functionality (components/ui/collapsible) rather than importing @radix-ui/react-collapsible everywhere. That makes future changes (styling wrappers, forwarding refs, adding defaults, swapping implementations, or adding TypeScript wrappers) trivial because callers only need to change this file. It also improves tree-shaking and code discoverability by keeping all UI primitives under components/ui.

Although tiny, this module is deliberate: it intentionally does not wrap the primitives with additional props or forward refs (it exports the primitives as-is). That keeps the file simple and avoids changing runtime behavior, but it also means certain conveniences (className merging, default variants, ref forwarding) are not provided here and must be handled where the primitives are consumed or in a future enhancement to this file.

key_constructs.name: Collapsible
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for CollapsiblePrimitive.Root — the root component that controls collapsible state
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a single import point for the collapsible root so callers can import from the app's UI layer instead of @radix-ui/react-collapsible; useful for future extension or swapping with a custom implementation.

key_constructs.name: CollapsibleTrigger
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for CollapsiblePrimitive.CollapsibleTrigger — the interactive element that toggles open/closed state
key_constructs.reasoning: Offers a named export for the trigger primitive, centralizing imports and making it easy to later augment triggers (styling, accessibility) in one place.

key_constructs.name: CollapsibleContent
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for CollapsiblePrimitive.CollapsibleContent — the collapsible content region that animates open/closed
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposes the content primitive through the app UI layer so content styling/animation defaults can be introduced later without changing call sites.

key_behaviors: provides a Root component to manage collapsible state
provides a Trigger component to toggle state
provides a Content component to render collapsible children

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or ignoring the "use client" directive
pitfalls.consequence: Radix primitives will attempt to run on the server or the component will be treated as a server component, causing runtime errors or missing interactivity.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the "use client" directive at the top of the file or wrap the usage in a client-only component.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming this file forwards refs or injects className/variants
pitfalls.consequence: Callers may rely on behavior (e.g., ref forwarding or default styles) that doesn't exist and encounter layout/accessibility issues or verbose duplicated code.
pitfalls.prevention: If you need default styling or ref forwarding, add explicit wrappers here (using forwardRef) rather than assuming it.

pitfalls.mistake: Importing @radix-ui/react-collapsible directly from other modules after creating this adapter
pitfalls.consequence: Inconsistent usage across the codebase and harder future swaps; defeats single-source-of-truth intent.
pitfalls.prevention: Enforce or prefer imports from components/ui/collapsible across the codebase.

components/ui/context-menu.tsx.short_description: React wrappers around Radix Context Menu primitives with Tailwind-based styling and icons.
components/ui/context-menu.tsx.description:
This file provides a thin, styled component layer over @radix-ui/react-context-menu primitives for use across the application. It centralizes styling, animation classes, and common small augmentations (like inset support and indicators) so that context menus in the app are visually consistent and easy to use. The wrappers are implemented as forwardRef components (where appropriate) to preserve native ref forwarding and to remain compatible with Radix’s primitives and external usage patterns.

The design purpose is to encapsulate Radix boilerplate (Content inside Portal, consistent className composition, icon indicators for checkbox/radio items, and a Chevron for sub-triggers) so application code can import readable semantic components (ContextMenuItem, ContextMenuCheckboxItem, ContextMenuSub, etc.) instead of dealing with Radix markup and classNames each time. The file depends on a local cn utility (classNames composition), Lucide icons for visuals, and Radix for behavior/aria/accessibility. It is marked with "use client" to ensure it runs as a client component in Next.js environments.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenu
key_constructs.purpose: Root component for context menus; directly re-exported from Radix as the top-level context menu controller.
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposing the Root primitive under a domain-specific name keeps imports consistent with the rest of the UI components and hides the Radix import everywhere else.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuTrigger
key_constructs.purpose: Trigger element for opening the context menu; re-exported from Radix.
key_constructs.reasoning: Providing the trigger primitive under the app's components namespace allows consistent imports and potential local customizations later without touch points across the codebase.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuContent
key_constructs.purpose: Styled Content wrapper which always renders inside a Radix Portal and applies animation and layout classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the common Content configuration—Portal placement, z-index, rounded border, shadows, animation data attributes—so every usage has consistent behavior and visuals.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuItem
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapper for Radix Item that adds base styling and supports an inset prop for visual alignment.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates repeated className patterns (focus styles, disabled styling, padding) and provides an optional inset to align items with checkbox/radio items that reserve left space for indicators.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuCheckboxItem
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapper for Radix CheckboxItem that includes an ItemIndicator rendering a Check icon and correct left spacing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Abstracts the checkbox indicator layout and icon so callers simply provide children and controlled props (like checked), reducing copy/paste and ensuring consistent indicator alignment.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuRadioItem
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapper for Radix RadioItem that includes a filled Circle indicator and correct left spacing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Same rationale as CheckboxItem: consistent radio indicator and spacing for all radio items in the app.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuSub
key_constructs.purpose: Re-export of Radix Sub primitive to create nested submenus.
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposes Radix Sub API through the app's components surface so submenus are available where needed, and styling wrappers for sub content/trigger are provided in the same file.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuSubTrigger
key_constructs.purpose: Submenu trigger that renders a ChevronRight icon and supports an inset prop; forwards refs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a prepared SubTrigger that visually indicates nested menus and supports the same inset alignment convention as regular items, so submenu triggers look consistent.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuSubContent
key_constructs.purpose: Styled wrapper for SubContent (submenu panel) with animation and layout classes; forwards refs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures submenu panels have the same visual language (shadows, borders, animations) as primary Content and keeps their layout behavior consistent.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuShortcut
key_constructs.purpose: Small presentational span for showing keyboard shortcuts on the right side of menu items.
key_constructs.reasoning: Extracts a common small-span element with whitespace and text styling so items can show the shortcut badge consistently (used as a child within items).

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuLabel
key_constructs.purpose: Label element for grouping menu items; supports inset alignment and heavier font.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to visually separate groups of menu items with a bolded label, preserving alignment with inset items.

key_constructs.name: ContextMenuSeparator
key_constructs.purpose: Visual separator between menu sections with margin and bg-border styling.
key_constructs.reasoning: Standardizes separators so spacing and color match the design system across context menus.

provides checkbox and radio menu items with indicators
supports nested submenus with chevrons
applies consistent styling and animations for all menu panels

pitfalls.consequence: Radix primitives rely on client-side event handling; removing 'use client' will break interactivity and refs.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep 'use client' at the top of the file for Next.js app router client components.

pitfalls.consequence: Menu open/close animations and slide directions will stop working or behave inconsistently.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve or carefully update the data-* animation class patterns and test menu open/close transitions.

pitfalls.mistake: Passing uncontrolled/controlled props incorrectly (e.g., misuse of checked on CheckboxItem without handling onCheckedChange)
pitfalls.consequence: The checkbox/radio items may not update UI as expected or may produce React warnings about switching between controlled/uncontrolled.
pitfalls.prevention: When using ContextMenuCheckboxItem or ContextMenuRadioItem, follow Radix controlled/uncontrolled semantics: provide checked and onCheckedChange for controlled usage or defaultChecked for uncontrolled usage.

pitfalls.mistake: Renaming or removing the cn utility import without replacing its functionality
pitfalls.consequence: ClassName composition may fail or lead to runtime errors.
pitfalls.prevention: If refactoring, ensure an equivalent classNames composition function is used and update imports accordingly.

components/ui/command.tsx.short_description: Reusable, styled React wrappers around cmdk primitives to provide a command palette/dialog UI.
components/ui/command.tsx.description:
This file provides a set of small React components that wrap the cmdk Command primitives and integrate them with the application's Dialog component and styling system. It exists to centralize the app's command-palette UI surface: consistent Tailwind-based styling, keyboard/interaction behavior coming from cmdk, and wiring into the existing Dialog (Radix-based) component so the command palette can be opened as a modal. Each wrapper is implemented with forwardRef to preserve ref forwarding and to keep the types aligned with cmdk primitives.

Design-wise the file is intentionally thin: it does not reimplement behavior but standardizes CSS classes, data-attribute selectors, and structure so consumers can compose a command palette with consistent visual and accessibility expectations. It uses small, focused components (Command, CommandDialog, CommandInput, CommandList, CommandEmpty, CommandGroup, CommandItem, CommandSeparator, CommandShortcut), each mapping 1:1 to a cmdk primitive or a tiny composition (CommandDialog wraps Dialog + DialogContent + Command). The module depends on the project's utility function cn for className merging and the Dialog/DialogContent components for modal behavior. The file must be used on the client ("use client") in Next.js projects because it contains interactive primitives and hooks into client-side behavior.

This file fits in the larger UI system as the canonical command palette implementation. Other pages or components will import these wrappers to build command items and groups without needing to remember the exact Tailwind classnames or to rewire the Dialog integration. It is also a single place to adjust the palette's look/feel (height, spacing, icon sizing) or accessibility tweaks.

key_constructs.name: Command
key_constructs.purpose: A forwardRef wrapper around CommandPrimitive applying consistent container styling.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the top-level command container appearance and ensures correct typing/ref forwarding so other components can render content inside a styled command surface.

key_constructs.name: CommandDialog
key_constructs.purpose: Composes Dialog and DialogContent with Command to present the command palette inside a modal dialog.
key_constructs.reasoning: Consumers simply render CommandDialog with DialogProps to open a full command palette modal; decouples dialog implementation from command primitives and avoids repeating dialog markup.

key_constructs.name: CommandInput
key_constructs.purpose: A composed input area that includes a search icon and styles an underlying cmdk input primitive.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures the input has consistent padding, icon placement and height, and reuses cmdk's input while preserving ref and props forwarding.

key_constructs.name: CommandList
key_constructs.purpose: Wraps the cmdk list primitive and enforces a max-height/scrolling behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Keeps the list scrollable and constrains size across the application so different command palettes behave similarly.

key_constructs.name: CommandEmpty
key_constructs.purpose: Wraps cmdk.Empty to provide a styled, centered empty-state message for the command palette.
key_constructs.reasoning: Standardizes how empty search results are shown (spacing, typography).

key_constructs.name: CommandGroup
key_constructs.purpose: Stylish wrapper around cmdk.Group for grouping related command items with heading styles.
key_constructs.reasoning: Consolidates group header appearance and spacing rules used for groups of commands.

key_constructs.name: CommandSeparator
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapper around the cmdk.Separator primitive to render thin dividers between groups/items.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a consistent separator style (height and color) used across command palettes.

key_constructs.name: CommandItem
key_constructs.purpose: A styled cmdk.Item that sets selection/disabled visuals and layout for individual commands.
key_constructs.reasoning: Captures common item behavior such as selected styling, disabled state handling, and icon sizing so consumers only supply content.

key_constructs.name: CommandShortcut
key_constructs.purpose: A small helper for rendering keyboard shortcuts on the right side of command items.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a consistent typographic and alignment style for shortcut labels (e.g., Cmd+K).

key_behaviors: renders a command palette inside a dialog
provides a styled searchable input with icon
groups and displays command items with selection/disabled states
constrains list height and shows empty state when needed

pitfalls.mistake: Treat this file as pure presentational CSS and move behaviors into other components.
pitfalls.consequence: You may break ref forwarding or lose cmdk behavior (keyboard navigation, selection) if props/ref are not forwarded correctly.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep forwardRef wrappers intact and preserve passing {...props} and ref to underlying cmdk primitives.

pitfalls.mistake: Rename or remove the custom attribute strings (e.g., cmdk-input-wrapper, cmdk-group-heading) used in the scoped Tailwind selectors.
pitfalls.consequence: Tailwind-based styling rules that target nested selectors will no longer match, causing layout/icon sizing to break.
pitfalls.prevention: If you change markup attributes/classnames update the corresponding complex selector strings in Command and other wrappers.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume this component is safe to render server-side.
pitfalls.consequence: Because of "use client" and interactive third-party primitives, rendering on the server can cause mismatch or runtime errors in Next.js.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep "use client" and ensure this file is only imported inside client components or pages that are client-side.

CommandInput: shows how the search icon and input are composed and sized
CommandItem: shows data attribute driven styles for selected/disabled states

related_files.reason_to_check: If Dialog internals or props change (e.g., how DialogContent className is applied), update CommandDialog to pass or adapt props accordingly.

related_files.relationship: The cn utility is used to merge conditional classNames across all wrappers.
related_files.reason_to_check: If cn implementation changes, className merging/behavior could be impacted and visual regressions may appear.

components/ui/dialog.tsx.short_description: A thin, styled wrapper around Radix UI Dialog primitives to provide a consistent modal/dialog API and Tailwind-based styling.
components/ui/dialog.tsx.description:
This file provides composed, styled React components built on top of @radix-ui/react-dialog primitives to standardize dialog (modal) usage across the app. It exists to centralize styling, accessibility affordances, and small behavior patterns (like a built-in close button) so other components can import and use a consistent Dialog API without repeating Tailwind classes or Radix glue logic. The file is client-only ("use client") because it uses forwardRef, hooks-ready primitives, and UI interactions that run in the browser.

Design decisions: each Radix primitive (Root, Trigger, Portal, Overlay, Content, Title, Description, Close) is either re-exported directly or wrapped to apply consistent styling and composition. Overlays and Content are wrapped with React.forwardRef to merge className via a local cn utility and to ensure proper ref forwarding. DialogContent composes DialogPortal and DialogOverlay and injects a Close button (lucide-react X icon) so consumers do not need to add that every time. Data-state-based Tailwind animation classes are used so Radix's state attributes trigger CSS animations.

How it fits: This is a presentational/ui primitives file used by any feature needing modal dialogs. It standardizes structure: Dialog (Root) + DialogTrigger + DialogPortal + DialogOverlay + DialogContent + DialogHeader/Footer/Title/Description + DialogClose. Because it centralizes style tokens and markup conventions (close button placement, responsive sizing, animations), changes here propagate to all dialog usages.

key_constructs.name: Dialog
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for DialogPrimitive.Root — the dialog root component representing the dialog state container.
key_constructs.reasoning: Exposes the Radix Root under a project-local name so other modules consistently import Dialog instead of the Radix primitive directly.

callers.context: import { Dialog, DialogContent, DialogHeader, DialogTitle } from "@/components/ui/dialog";

key_constructs.name: DialogTrigger
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for DialogPrimitive.Trigger — element that opens the dialog when interacted with.
key_constructs.reasoning: Keeps API surface aligned with local naming and allows swapping or extending trigger behavior in one place if needed.

key_constructs.name: DialogPortal
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for DialogPrimitive.Portal — portals content to the document body.
key_constructs.reasoning: Portal usage is encapsulated so Content can compose it and maintain consistent DOM layering.

key_constructs.name: DialogClose
key_constructs.purpose: Alias for DialogPrimitive.Close — an interactive element to close the dialog.
key_constructs.reasoning: Re-exported for convenience to consumers who want a close control outside of the provided Content's built-in one.

key_constructs.name: DialogOverlay
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapped Overlay primitive that adds consistent backdrop styling, z-index, and animation classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: ForwardRef wrapper centralizes overlay classes and ensures className merging via cn so overlay style changes are global.

key_constructs.name: DialogContent
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapped Content primitive that composes Portal + Overlay, provides layout, animations, and an included Close button with icon.
key_constructs.reasoning: Most dialogs need the portal + overlay + content + close button; combining them reduces duplication and enforces a consistent structure and accessibility patterns.

key_constructs.name: DialogHeader
key_constructs.purpose: Simple layout wrapper for dialog header area (title/description), applying spacing and text alignment.
key_constructs.reasoning: Small convenience component to keep content markup consistent across dialogs.

key_constructs.name: DialogFooter
key_constructs.purpose: Layout wrapper for dialog actions (buttons) with responsive ordering and spacing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encourages consistent placement and ordering of action buttons (primary on the right on larger screens).

key_constructs.name: DialogTitle
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapped Title primitive with standardized typography classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures dialog titles share consistent font-size and weight across the app; forwarded ref preserves Radix accessibility hooks.

key_constructs.name: DialogDescription
key_constructs.purpose: Wrapped Description primitive for consistent smaller, muted text below the title.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes description style and ensures proper ref forwarding for accessibility.

key_behaviors: provide a drop-in Dialog component API consistent across the app
open and close modals via Radix primitives
render dialog content in a portal with an overlay
inject a default close button positioned at the top-right
merge custom className props with default Tailwind classes via cn

pitfalls.mistake: Remove or forget the "use client" directive
pitfalls.consequence: React client-side-only behaviors (forwardRefs, event handlers, lucide icon usage) may break in Next.js server components or cause hydration errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep "use client" at top of file when editing or moving this component; if converting to server component, ensure all client-only dependencies are replaced.

pitfalls.mistake: Directly changing Radix data-state class names or animation tokens without understanding Radix state attributes
pitfalls.consequence: Animations or open/close transitions may stop working or produce inconsistent styling across dialogs.
pitfalls.prevention: Update both the Radix usage and the CSS/Tailwind classes together; test both opening and closing transitions on mobile/desktop.

pitfalls.mistake: Assume DialogPrimitive.* components always have displayName set
pitfalls.consequence: Setting displayName to DialogPrimitive.Content.displayName may be undefined in some Radix versions (harmless but confusing for debugging).
pitfalls.prevention: If you need displayName for debugging or tooling, set an explicit string instead of copying from the primitive, e.g., DialogContent.displayName = 'DialogContent'.

DialogOverlay: overlay styling and data-state animations
DialogHeader/DialogFooter: small layout helpers for content authors
skip_unless_needed: the simple re-exports (Dialog, DialogTrigger, DialogPortal, DialogClose) — these are thin aliases
